name: sqlmap
description: SQLMap is the gold standard for automated SQL injection detection and exploitation. It supports all major SQL database backends (MySQL, PostgreSQL, Oracle, MSSQL, SQLite) and can perform complete database takeover through injection vulnerabilities. From data extraction to shell access, SQLMap automates what would take hours of manual effort into minutes of runtime.
how_it_works: SQLMap works by sending crafted HTTP requests with SQL payload variations to the target parameter. It uses a technique called 'boolean-based blind' (observing TRUE/FALSE response differences), 'time-based blind' (measuring response delays), 'error-based' (parsing DB error messages), 'UNION query-based' (appending UNION SELECT), and 'stacked queries' (executing multiple statements). It maintains a state machine that tracks which techniques succeed and progressively escalates from detection to data extraction, then to OS shell access via database-specific mechanisms like MySQL's INTO OUTFILE or PostgreSQL's COPY TO PROGRAM.
usage_scenarios: Testing web applications for SQL injection during penetration tests. Extracting credentials, credit card numbers, or PII from vulnerable databases. Demonstrating the impact of input validation failures to development teams. Automating SQL injection testing across large application portfolios.
strengths: Supports all 6 SQL injection techniques automatically
Can escalate from data extraction to OS shell
Massive database of injection payloads and tamper scripts
Session resumption and batch mode for automation
weaknesses: Noisy - generates thousands of requests that WAFs can detect
Can crash databases with aggressive settings (--risk=3)
False positives on complex applications with non-standard responses
Steep learning curve for advanced features and tamper scripts
comparison: jSQL Injection offers a GUI alternative but lacks SQLMap's depth and technique coverage. NoSQLMap targets NoSQL databases (MongoDB, CouchDB) which SQLMap ignores. bbqsql focuses on blind SQL injection specifically but is far less capable overall. SQLninja is MSSQL-specific and largely deprecated.

name: nikto
description: Nikto is a venerable web server scanner that checks for dangerous files, outdated software, and misconfigurations. It maintains one of the largest databases of web server fingerprints and vulnerability signatures, testing for over 6,700 potentially dangerous files and programs, outdated versions of software on hundreds of servers, and version-specific problems.
how_it_works: Nikto operates by sending HTTP requests to the target server and comparing responses against its extensive database of vulnerability signatures. It iterates through its database of checks, testing for known vulnerable CGI scripts, default files that should have been removed, outdated server software, and configuration errors. It uses LibWhisker for HTTP request formatting and supports SSL, proxy authentication, and various HTTP methods. The scanner also performs basic fingerprinting to identify the server software and adjust its checks accordingly.
usage_scenarios: Initial reconnaissance of web servers during penetration tests. Checking for default credentials and forgotten administrative interfaces. Verifying that security patches have been applied. Compliance scanning for known vulnerable components.
strengths: Fast initial scanning for low-hanging fruit
Output in multiple formats (HTML, CSV, XML)
weaknesses: Extremely noisy - easily detected by IDS/WAF
Many false positives on modern applications
No JavaScript rendering - misses SPA vulnerabilities
Signature-based only, no intelligent vulnerability logic
comparison: OpenVAS provides more comprehensive vulnerability scanning including network-level checks but is heavier. WPScan specializes in WordPress and provides deeper CMS-specific testing. WhatWeb focuses on fingerprinting rather than vulnerability detection. Arachni is a full web application scanner with crawling and JS rendering that goes beyond Nikto's signature approach.

name: dirb
description: DIRB is a web content scanner that brute-forces directory and file names on web servers using custom wordlists. Unlike directory brute-forcers that only check HTTP status codes, DIRB analyzes response content to distinguish between real pages and custom error pages, making it effective against servers that return 200 for everything.
how_it_works: DIRB works by iterating through a wordlist and appending each entry to the target URL, then sending HTTP requests and analyzing the responses. Its key innovation is response content analysis - it doesn't just rely on HTTP status codes. If a server returns 200 for all requests (common with catch-all rewrite rules), DIRB compares response sizes and content patterns to identify genuinely different responses. It also supports custom extensions, authentication, cookies, and headers for more targeted scanning.
usage_scenarios: Discovering hidden directories and files on web servers. Finding backup files, configuration files, and administrative interfaces. Enumerating API endpoints on REST services. Testing for exposed version control directories (.git, .svn).
strengths: Lightweight and fast compared to alternatives
Multiple built-in wordlists for different scenarios
Supports custom headers, cookies, and authentication
weaknesses: Single-threaded - slower than modern alternatives
Limited HTTP method support
No JavaScript rendering for SPA applications
Wordlist quality directly impacts results
comparison: Gobuster is significantly faster due to multi-threading but relies only on status codes. Feroxbuster is the modern Rust-based alternative with recursive scanning and wildcard detection. Dirsearch offers similar functionality with Python extensibility. ffuf is the most versatile, supporting multiple fuzzing modes beyond directory discovery.

name: gobuster
description: Gobuster is a high-speed directory and DNS brute-forcing tool written in Go. Its multi-threaded architecture makes it orders of magnitude faster than Python-based alternatives like DIRB, and it supports multiple modes: directory/file enumeration, DNS subdomain enumeration, and virtual host name discovery.
how_it_works: Gobuster leverages Go's concurrency model to spawn dozens or hundreds of goroutines that simultaneously send HTTP requests. Each goroutine reads from a shared wordlist channel and sends requests independently, aggregating results. It supports pattern-based extension fuzzing (appending .php, .html, etc.), custom status code filtering, and response size filtering. For DNS mode, it performs actual DNS resolution rather than HTTP requests, and for vhost mode, it sends requests with varying Host headers to detect virtual hosting configurations.
usage_scenarios: Rapid directory enumeration during time-constrained engagements. DNS subdomain discovery for large organizations. Virtual host enumeration on shared hosting. API endpoint fuzzing with custom patterns.
strengths: Multiple modes: dir, dns, vhost, fuzz
weaknesses: No content analysis - relies on status codes only
Can overwhelm slower servers or trigger rate limiting
No recursive directory discovery
Limited output format options
comparison: ffuf is similarly fast with more fuzzing flexibility and recursive mode. Feroxbuster adds recursive scanning and content-based filtering. DIRB is much slower but performs content analysis. Wfuzz supports parameter fuzzing that Gobuster cannot do.

name: commix
description: Commix is an automated command injection detection and exploitation tool. While SQLMap focuses on SQL injection, Commix targets OS command injection vulnerabilities, allowing testers to detect and exploit instances where user input is passed unsanitized to system shell commands.
how_it_works: Commix identifies command injection by injecting shell metacharacters (semicolon, pipe, backtick, dollar sign, etc.) into target parameters and analyzing responses for evidence of command execution. It supports multiple injection techniques: classic (appending commands with semicolons), eval-based (injecting into eval() functions), time-based (measuring sleep delays), file-based (writing output to accessible files), and temp-file-based. For blind injection, it uses boolean and time-based techniques similar to SQLMap's approach, but for shell commands instead of SQL queries.
usage_scenarios: Testing web applications for OS command injection during penetration tests. Exploiting command injection to gain shell access on the underlying server. Demonstrating the impact of unsafe input handling in system calls. Verifying that input sanitization properly filters shell metacharacters.
strengths: The most comprehensive command injection testing tool available
Can escalate to OS shell access
Compatible with SQLMap's session and tamper script approach
weaknesses: Limited to command injection - doesn't test for code injection or SSTI
False positives on applications with complex output parsing
Less maintained than SQLMap with fewer community contributions
WAF evasion capabilities are less mature
comparison: SQLMap is the equivalent for SQL injection. Template injection tools (tplmap, SSTImap) cover server-side template injection. Commix is unique in its focus on OS command injection - no other tool provides the same depth of automated testing for this vulnerability class.

name: wpscan
description: WPScan is the definitive WordPress security scanner. It specializes in enumerating WordPress installations, identifying themes, plugins, and users, and detecting known vulnerabilities in all discovered components. It queries the WPScan Vulnerability Database which maintains a comprehensive index of WordPress core, plugin, and theme vulnerabilities.
how_it_works: WPScan first identifies the WordPress installation and version through fingerprinting (checking meta generators, RSS feeds, and static file hashes). It then enumerates installed themes by checking known theme paths and file hashes. Plugin enumeration works similarly, iterating through a database of known plugin directory structures. User enumeration exploits WordPress's author archive pages and REST API endpoints. Each discovered component is checked against the WPScan Vulnerability Database API for known vulnerabilities, CVEs, and exploit references.
usage_scenarios: Security auditing of WordPress sites. Enumerating users for brute force attacks. Identifying vulnerable plugins before they're exploited. Compliance checking for WordPress security best practices.
strengths: Supports authenticated scanning with cookie or password
API integration for real-time vulnerability data
weaknesses: WordPress-specific - useless for other CMS platforms
API rate limits on free tier (50 requests/week)
Aggressive mode is very noisy and easily detected
Cannot detect custom plugin vulnerabilities not in its database
comparison: Joomscan provides similar functionality for Joomla. DrupalScan targets Drupal installations. Nikto can detect some WordPress issues but lacks the depth of WPScan's WordPress-specific knowledge. CMSmap supports WordPress, Joomla, and Drupal but with less depth than WPScan.

name: xsser
description: XSSer is an automated cross-site scripting (XSS) injection framework that tests web applications for reflected, stored, and DOM-based XSS vulnerabilities. It includes a comprehensive payload encoder that can bypass common WAF filters and input sanitization mechanisms.
how_it_works: XSSer works by injecting XSS payloads into all discoverable input vectors (URL parameters, forms, headers) and analyzing responses for evidence of successful injection. It maintains a database of XSS payloads organized by injection context (HTML attribute, JavaScript, CSS, URL) and encoding method. The tool supports automatic payload encoding using URL encoding, Unicode encoding, HTML entity encoding, and combinations thereof to bypass filters. For DOM-based XSS, it uses static HTML analysis and dynamic testing with a headless browser to detect sinks and sources.
usage_scenarios: Testing web applications for XSS vulnerabilities during security assessments. Bypassing WAF filters through payload encoding and obfuscation. Validating XSS remediation after fixes. Automated XSS scanning across large application portfolios.
strengths: Comprehensive XSS payload database with encoding options
Supports reflected, stored, and DOM-based XSS testing
Multiple WAF bypass techniques built in
GTK GUI available for visual testing
weaknesses: High false positive rate without manual verification
DOM-based XSS detection is less reliable than reflected/stored
No JavaScript rendering for SPA applications
Cannot test authenticated areas easily without complex cookie setup
comparison: Dalfox is a modern alternative with better performance and JSON output. XSStrike uses intelligent payload generation based on response analysis. BruteXSS focuses on brute-force parameter fuzzing. OWASP ZAP provides XSS testing as part of its broader scanning capabilities.

name: burpsuite
description: Burp Suite Professional is the industry-standard web application security testing platform. It combines an intercepting proxy, vulnerability scanner, and extensive toolkit for manual and automated testing. While not open-source, the community edition is included in BlackArch for its proxy and manual testing capabilities.
how_it_works: Burp Suite operates as an HTTP/S proxy that intercepts all traffic between the browser and target application. The Proxy module captures and allows modification of every request and response. The Scanner (Pro only) performs active and passive vulnerability analysis by crawling the application and injecting test payloads. Repeater enables manual request manipulation. Intruder performs automated payload delivery for fuzzing and brute-forcing. Decoder handles encoding/decoding. Comparer diffs responses. The Extender API allows custom BApp extensions.
usage_scenarios: Manual penetration testing of web applications with full request control. Automated vulnerability scanning of complex applications. API security testing and parameter fuzzing. Session analysis and authentication testing.
strengths: Industry standard with unmatched feature set
Seamless workflow between manual and automated testing
weaknesses: Pro version requires expensive license
Java-based - resource heavy and slower startup
Community edition lacks the automated scanner
Learning curve for advanced features
comparison: OWASP ZAP is the free/open-source alternative with similar proxy capabilities. Fiddler is Windows-focused but offers comparable proxy features. mitmproxy provides a Python-based console alternative for programmatic interception. Caido is a modern Rust-based alternative with a fresh UI.

name: zap
description: OWASP ZAP (Zed Attack Proxy) is the world's most widely used free web application security scanner. It provides automated scanners and a set of manual tools for finding vulnerabilities in web applications, serving as both an intercepting proxy and an automated vulnerability scanner.
how_it_works: ZAP functions as a man-in-the-middle proxy, intercepting and logging all HTTP/S traffic. Its Active Scanner crawls the application, identifies input vectors, and injects attack payloads to detect vulnerabilities. The Passive Scanner analyzes traffic without sending any new requests, detecting information leakage, weak headers, and cookie issues. The spider crawls the application's structure, and the Ajax spider uses a headless browser for dynamic content. ZAP also includes fuzzing, forced browsing, and authentication handling capabilities.
usage_scenarios: Free web application security scanning for organizations without commercial tool budgets. Automated regression testing in CI/CD pipelines via ZAP's API. Manual testing with the intercepting proxy. Security training and education.
strengths: Full API for automation and CI/CD integration
Active community and marketplace of add-ons
Both automated and manual testing capabilities
weaknesses: Scanner less accurate than commercial alternatives
Java-based - resource intensive
Complex configuration for advanced scanning
Report generation less polished than commercial tools
comparison: Burp Suite Professional offers superior scanning accuracy and workflow but costs thousands. Arachni provides similar open-source scanning but is less maintained. w3af offers comprehensive scanning but has reliability issues. Nikto focuses on signature-based scanning without proxy capabilities.

name: wfuzz
description: Wfuzz is a web application brute-forcing tool that allows fuzzing of any part of an HTTP request - URL, headers, POST data, cookies, and more. It uses a simple but powerful syntax where 'FUZZ' markers in any request field are replaced with payload values from wordlists.
how_it_works: Wfuzz operates by parsing a request template that contains one or more 'FUZZ' markers and replacing each marker with values from specified wordlists. Each unique combination generates a new HTTP request. It supports multiple simultaneous fuzzing points with independent wordlists, recursive fuzzing, and response filtering by status code, word count, line count, or regex. The plugin architecture supports custom encoders, iterators, and output processors.
usage_scenarios: Fuzzing parameters that other tools can't reach (custom headers, cookies). Directory and file brute-forcing. Parameter discovery and injection testing. Authentication brute-forcing with custom request formats.
strengths: Can fuzz ANY part of an HTTP request
Multiple simultaneous fuzzing points with different wordlists
weaknesses: Slower than modern alternatives like ffuf
Complex syntax for advanced use cases
No recursive discovery mode
Limited built-in encoding/payload generation
comparison: ffuf is faster and supports more fuzzing categories but has less flexible request templating. Gobuster is faster for directory/DNS but can't fuzz arbitrary request fields. Arjun specializes in parameter discovery. Wfuzz's strength is its universal fuzzing capability across any HTTP request component.

name: ffuf
description: Feroxbuster-derived Fuzz Faster U Fool (ffuf) is a fast web fuzzer written in Go that supports directory discovery, parameter fuzzing, subdomain enumeration, and custom HTTP request fuzzing. Its speed and flexibility have made it the go-to fuzzing tool for modern penetration testers.
how_it_works: ffuf uses Go's concurrency primitives to manage a configurable pool of goroutines that simultaneously process wordlist entries. Each worker reads from a shared input channel, constructs an HTTP request by replacing 'FUZZ' keywords, sends the request, and filters the response based on user-specified criteria (status code, size, word count, line count, regex). It supports multiple FUZZ keywords with different wordlists, recursive directory scanning, rate limiting, and automatic calibration to detect custom error pages.
usage_scenarios: High-speed directory and file discovery. Subdomain enumeration via DNS resolution. Parameter name fuzzing on API endpoints. Virtual host discovery through Host header manipulation.
strengths: Extremely fast - fastest directory fuzzer available
Multiple fuzzing modes with FUZZ keyword system
Built-in filtering and calibration for custom 404s
weaknesses: Go binary - not as extensible as Python tools
Limited payload encoding options compared to Wfuzz
No built-in vulnerability detection - pure fuzzer
Can overwhelm targets if rate limiting isn't configured
comparison: Gobuster is similarly fast but less flexible with fuzzing modes. Feroxbuster offers recursive scanning with content-based filtering. Wfuzz has more flexible request templating. Dirsearch is slower but easier to use for simple cases.

name: arachni
description: Arachni is a feature-full web application security scanner that provides intelligent crawling, JavaScript execution, and comprehensive vulnerability detection. Unlike signature-based scanners, Arachni uses a combination of pattern matching, taint analysis, and behavioral analysis to minimize false positives.
how_it_works: Arachni starts by crawling the target application using a browser emulator that executes JavaScript to discover dynamically-generated content. It identifies all input vectors (forms, links, headers, cookies) and systematically injects probe payloads. For each vulnerability class, it uses specialized detection logic: SQL injection detection uses differential testing with multiple payloads, XSS detection uses taint tracking to follow input from source to output, and CSRF detection analyzes form submissions for missing anti-CSRF tokens. Each finding is verified through multiple checks to reduce false positives before reporting.
usage_scenarios: Comprehensive web application security assessment. Automated scanning with high accuracy requirements. CI/CD pipeline integration via the REST API. Large-scale scanning of application portfolios.
commands: arachni_rest  # Start the REST API server
strengths: Low false positive rate through verification
REST API for automation and scripting
weaknesses: Slow compared to lightweight scanners
Ruby-based - heavy memory usage
Project less actively maintained in recent years
Configuration can be complex for custom scan profiles
comparison: OWASP ZAP is more actively maintained and has better community support. Burp Suite Professional offers superior workflow and scanning accuracy. w3af has similar capabilities but with reliability issues. Nikto is faster but purely signature-based with higher false positives.

name: skipfish
description: Skipfish is an active web application security reconnaissance tool developed by Google. It performs recursive crawling and dictionary-based probes to detect a wide range of security flaws, producing a detailed interactive HTML report with findings organized by severity.
how_it_works: Skipfish uses a high-performance HTTP crawler that recursively discovers the application's structure. It employs adaptive dictionary-based probing, starting with common paths and adapting its wordlist based on discovered technology stacks. The scanner checks for common vulnerabilities including injection flaws, XSS, directory traversal, authentication issues, and mixed content problems. Its unique feature is the adaptive learning mode where it calibrates itself against the target's response patterns, learning to distinguish between genuine errors and custom error pages.
usage_scenarios: Rapid security assessment of web applications. Pre-deployment security scanning in development environments. Batch scanning of multiple sites. Generating actionable reports for development teams.
strengths: Extremely fast - one of the fastest web scanners
Adaptive dictionary that learns from target responses
Low false positive rate through adaptive calibration
weaknesses: Not actively maintained since 2012
No JavaScript rendering for SPA applications
Limited vulnerability detection compared to modern scanners
C-only codebase difficult to extend
comparison: Arachni provides deeper scanning with JS rendering but is much slower. OWASP ZAP offers more features and active maintenance. Nikto has a larger signature database but is slower and noisier. Nuclei offers similar speed with modern YAML-based templates.

name: w3af
description: w3af (Web Application Attack and Audit Framework) is a comprehensive web application security scanner that combines crawling, vulnerability detection, and exploitation capabilities in a single framework. It supports over 200 plugins organized into discovery, audit, bruteforce, and exploitation categories.
how_it_works: w3af uses a plugin architecture where different phases of testing are handled by specialized plugins. Discovery plugins crawl the application and identify URLs, forms, and input vectors. Audit plugins test each input vector for specific vulnerability classes (SQL injection, XSS, CSRF, etc.). Bruteforce plugins test default credentials on discovered login forms. Exploit plugins attempt to leverage discovered vulnerabilities. The framework manages the workflow between these phases, passing discovered information from one plugin to the next for comprehensive coverage.
usage_scenarios: Full web application security assessment with exploitation. Plugin-based custom testing workflows. Automated scanning with detailed reporting. Integration with custom security testing pipelines.
weaknesses: Prone to crashes and stability issues
Slow compared to modern alternatives
Python 2 codebase with limited Python 3 support
Complex configuration for effective scanning
comparison: OWASP ZAP is more stable and actively maintained. Burp Suite Professional offers a superior workflow. Arachni provides similar depth with better accuracy. w3af's main advantage is its integrated exploitation capabilities.

name: dotdotpwn
description: DotDotPwn is a directory traversal fuzzer specifically designed to find path traversal vulnerabilities in web applications, FTP servers, and other services. It can test for directory traversal using multiple encoding schemes and evasion techniques.
how_it_works: DotDotPwn systematically injects path traversal payloads (../, ..%2f, ..%5c, and encoded variants) into target parameters. It supports multiple protocols: HTTP/HTTPS (GET and POST parameters), FTP (CWD commands), SMTP, and raw TCP. For each injection, it checks the response for indicators of successful traversal, such as the contents of /etc/passwd or other known files. The tool includes a comprehensive payload list with various encoding and obfuscation techniques to bypass WAFs and input sanitization.
usage_scenarios: Testing web applications for directory traversal vulnerabilities. Auditing FTP servers for path traversal in CWD commands. Checking custom protocols for path injection. Validating path sanitization in file handling code.
strengths: Specialized focus on directory traversal - the best at it
Multiple protocol support (HTTP, FTP, SMTP, raw TCP)
Extensive encoding and evasion payload library
Configurable traversal depth and file targets
weaknesses: One-trick pony - only tests for path traversal
Perl-based - dependencies can be problematic
No GUI or modern output formats
Limited to known file detection (can't detect arbitrary file access)
comparison: SQLMap covers SQL injection comprehensively while DotDotPwn covers path traversal. Burp Suite Professional includes path traversal testing as part of its scanner. Custom Python scripts can test specific traversal scenarios but lack DotDotPwn's payload breadth. No other dedicated directory traversal tool matches its coverage.

name: padbuster
description: PadBuster is a tool for automating padding oracle attacks against web applications. It exploits CBC mode encryption implementations that improperly reveal information about padding validity, allowing decryption and encryption of data without knowing the key.
how_it_works: PadBuster exploits the padding oracle vulnerability in CBC-mode encryption. In CBC mode, each block of plaintext is XORed with the previous ciphertext block before encryption. A padding oracle exists when an application returns different error messages (or timing differences) for invalid padding versus valid padding. PadBuster systematically manipulates ciphertext bytes, sending modified requests and observing the oracle's response. By determining when padding is valid (PKCS#7), it can deduce the intermediate state and thus decrypt the plaintext. It can also craft valid ciphertext for arbitrary plaintext, enabling encryption without the key.
usage_scenarios: Exploiting padding oracle vulnerabilities in web application encryption. Decrypting authentication tokens that use CBC-mode encryption. Modifying encrypted session data without knowing the key. Testing encryption implementations for oracle leakage.
strengths: Automates the complex padding oracle attack process
Supports both decryption and encryption operations
Multiple encoding options (base64, hex, URL encoding)
weaknesses: Padding oracle must be present - tool can't create the vulnerability
Slow - requires many requests per block
Error handling for custom oracle responses is tricky
No built-in rate limiting to avoid detection
comparison: poisoned-padding is a newer alternative with better output. Oracle attacks can also be performed manually with custom scripts. Burp Suite's padding oracle plugin provides a GUI approach. PadBuster remains the most well-known and widely-used tool for this specific attack.

name: httrack
description: HTTrack is a website copier/mirror tool that downloads entire websites from the Internet to a local directory, recursively building all directories, HTML, images, and other files from the server. While primarily a website mirroring tool, it's invaluable for offline analysis during penetration tests.
how_it_works: HTTrack works by parsing HTML pages, extracting all links (href, src, action attributes), and recursively downloading linked content. It respects robots.txt by default (can be overridden), manages bandwidth throttling, and can resume interrupted downloads. The engine handles relative and absolute URLs, converts links for local browsing, and maintains the original site structure. It supports authentication, proxy configuration, and custom headers for accessing protected content.
usage_scenarios: Creating offline copies of target websites for analysis without alerting the target. Mirroring web applications for source code review. Archiving websites for evidence preservation. Downloading web content for offline vulnerability analysis.
strengths: Complete website mirroring with link conversion
weaknesses: Doesn't execute JavaScript - misses dynamic content
Can create very large downloads for media-heavy sites
No vulnerability scanning capability
May trigger rate limiting on protected sites
comparison: wget with mirror mode (-m) provides basic mirroring but less link conversion. Scrapoxy adds proxy rotation for anonymity. WebCopy is Windows-only. Cyotek WebCopy offers a GUI alternative. HTTrack's strength is its thorough link conversion for offline browsing.

name: whatweb
description: WhatWeb is a web scanner that identifies websites, CMS platforms, blogging platforms, statistic/analytics packages, JavaScript libraries, web servers, and embedded devices. It recognizes over 1,800 web technologies through fingerprinting.
how_it_works: WhatWeb uses a plugin architecture where each plugin identifies a specific technology. Plugins analyze HTTP responses using various detection methods: text pattern matching (looking for specific strings in HTML), URL pattern matching, hash matching (comparing file hashes), and HTTP header analysis. Aggressive mode downloads additional files (robots.txt, favicon, crossdomain.xml) for deeper fingerprinting. The tool supports recursion and can follow redirects, and can be configured for varying levels of stealth.
usage_scenarios: Reconnaissance of web technologies during penetration tests. Identifying CMS versions for vulnerability research. Technology stack fingerprinting for attack planning. Large-scale web survey and categorization.
strengths: Fast scanning with bulk input support
weaknesses: Doesn't detect vulnerabilities - only identifies technologies
Aggressive mode can be detected
Signatures can become outdated
Limited JavaScript analysis for SPA frameworks
comparison: Wappalyzer (browser extension) provides similar fingerprinting in a GUI. BuiltWith offers commercial technology identification. Nikto includes some fingerprinting but focuses on vulnerabilities. BlindElephant focused on version fingerprinting but is largely deprecated.

name: joomscan
description: JoomScan (OWASP Joomla Vulnerability Scanner) is a specialized tool for detecting security vulnerabilities in Joomla CMS installations. It enumerates components, identifies version information, and checks for known vulnerabilities.
how_it_works: JoomScan identifies the Joomla installation and version through fingerprinting techniques (checking specific files, meta tags, and MD5 hashes of known files). It then enumerates installed components, modules, and templates by probing known directory structures and file paths specific to Joomla. Each discovered component is checked against a database of known vulnerabilities. The scanner also tests for common Joomla misconfigurations including exposed administrative interfaces, debug mode, and default credentials.
usage_scenarios: Security auditing of Joomla-based websites. Pre-attack reconnaissance of Joomla installations. Vulnerability assessment of Joomla components. Checking Joomla configuration best practices.
strengths: Specialized for Joomla - deep CMS knowledge
Component enumeration for targeted vulnerability research
weaknesses: Joomla-specific - useless for other CMS platforms
Perl dependencies can be problematic
Vulnerability database less comprehensive than commercial alternatives
No exploitation capabilities
comparison: WPScan provides similar functionality for WordPress. CMSmap supports Joomla but with less depth. Nikto detects some Joomla issues but is not specialized. droopescan covers Joomla, WordPress, Drupal, and SilverStripe but with less depth per CMS.

name: droopescan
description: Droopescan is a plugin-based scanner that targets Drupal, SilverStripe, WordPress, and Joomla CMS installations. It specializes in version identification and plugin/theme enumeration with a focus on speed and accuracy.
how_it_works: Droopescan uses a plugin architecture where each CMS has a dedicated scanner module. Each module performs version fingerprinting by checking specific file hashes, changelogs, and meta information. Plugin and theme enumeration is done by probing known directory paths and checking for the presence of specific files. The scanner identifies potential vulnerabilities by correlating discovered versions with known CVE databases. It uses asynchronous requests for speed and supports proxy chains for stealthy scanning.
usage_scenarios: Multi-CMS scanning in environments with mixed platforms. Quick identification of CMS versions for vulnerability research. Enumerating plugins and themes across multiple CMS types. Automated scanning with structured JSON output for tool integration.
weaknesses: Less depth than CMS-specific tools like WPScan
Vulnerability database not as comprehensive
Limited exploitation capabilities
Some CMS modules less maintained than others
comparison: WPScan is deeper for WordPress-only testing. JoomScan is deeper for Joomla-only testing. CMSmap offers similar multi-CMS support. Droopescan's advantage is speed and structured output for automation.

name: tplmap
description: Tplmap is a server-side template injection (SSTI) detection and exploitation tool. It automates the process of detecting and exploiting template injection vulnerabilities across multiple template engines including Jinja2, Mako, Twig, Jade, Velocity, and more.
how_it_works: Tplmap works by injecting template expression payloads (like {{7*7}}, ${7*7}, <%= 7*7 %>) into target parameters and analyzing responses for evidence of server-side evaluation. When a template expression is evaluated, the response will contain the computed result (49) instead of the literal expression. Once injection is confirmed, Tplmap identifies the template engine by testing engine-specific syntax and error messages. It then uses engine-specific exploitation techniques to achieve remote code execution through the template engine's built-in functions or object traversal chains (like Python's __import__('os').popen('id'))
usage_scenarios: Testing web applications for server-side template injection. Exploiting SSTI to gain remote code execution. Identifying which template engine is in use. Validating SSTI remediation after fixes.
strengths: Can escalate to OS shell through SSTI
Multiple injection contexts (URL, POST, headers, cookies)
weaknesses: SSTI is relatively rare compared to other injection types
Engine-specific payloads may not work with sandboxed templates
Limited WAF evasion capabilities
Less actively maintained than SQLMap
comparison: SSTImap is a newer alternative with similar capabilities. SQLMap covers SQL injection but not SSTI. Commix covers command injection but not template injection. Tplmap is the most comprehensive dedicated SSTI tool.

name: gravscan
description: GravScan is a specialized scanner for Grav CMS installations. It identifies version information, detects installed plugins and themes, and checks for known vulnerabilities in the Grav CMS platform.
how_it_works: GravScan probes the target for Grav-specific files and directories (like /user/, /system/, /pages/, and configuration files). It identifies the Grav version through changelog files, meta generators, and specific file hashes. Plugin and theme enumeration is performed by checking known paths and analyzing the site's source code for Grav-specific markers. Each discovered component is checked against a database of known Grav vulnerabilities and CVEs.
usage_scenarios: Security assessment of Grav CMS installations. Version identification for vulnerability research. Plugin enumeration for attack surface mapping. Checking Grav configuration for security best practices.
weaknesses: Very niche - only useful for Grav CMS
Limited vulnerability database
Less maintained than CMS scanners for popular platforms
comparison: WPScan, JoomScan, and Droopescan target more popular CMS platforms. GravScan fills a niche for the less common Grav CMS. Generic web scanners like Nikto can detect some Grav issues but lack CMS-specific depth.

name: cmsmap
description: CMSmap is a Python-based CMS scanner that supports WordPress, Joomla, and Drupal. It automates the detection of CMS type, version enumeration, plugin detection, and vulnerability lookup for these three major content management systems.
how_it_works: CMSmap first identifies which CMS is running through fingerprinting techniques specific to each platform. Once identified, it enumerates the version through meta generators, specific file contents, and hash comparison. It then performs plugin/theme enumeration by probing known directory structures and checking for the presence of readme.txt, changelog, and other indicator files. Discovered components are checked against a local vulnerability database for known issues.
usage_scenarios: Quick CMS identification and scanning. Multi-platform CMS security assessment. Automated vulnerability scanning for WordPress, Joomla, and Drupal. Pre-engagement reconnaissance of CMS-based targets.
weaknesses: Less depth than CMS-specific tools
Vulnerability database less comprehensive
No exploitation capabilities
Less actively maintained than WPScan
comparison: WPScan is superior for WordPress-only testing. JoomScan is deeper for Joomla. Droopescan is faster with better output format. CMSmap's advantage is simplicity and multi-CMS support in one tool.

name: weevely
description: Weevely is a stealth PHP web shell generator and handler that creates obfuscated backdoors and provides a terminal-like interface for post-exploitation through the web shell. It generates polymorphic PHP payloads that evade signature-based detection.
how_it_works: Weevely generates PHP web shells using polymorphic code generation - each generated shell is syntactically unique but functionally identical, making signature-based detection nearly impossible. The generated PHP uses encoding, variable name randomization, and code obfuscation techniques. Communication between the client and the web shell uses HTTP(S) with encrypted payloads, making traffic analysis difficult. The handler provides over 30 post-exploitation modules for file operations, system command execution, database access, network tunneling, and privilege escalation.
usage_scenarios: Maintaining persistent access to compromised web servers. Post-exploitation through web shells. File management on compromised systems. Network pivoting through web shell proxies.
strengths: Stealthy traffic that blends with normal web traffic
weaknesses: PHP-only - no support for other languages
Web shell must be uploaded to target first
Active scans can detect the shell file
Limited compared to full C2 frameworks like Metasploit
comparison: b374k and WSO are simpler PHP shells without encryption. China Chopper is minimal but widely detected. Metasploit's meterpreter provides more powerful post-exploitation but requires different delivery. Weevely's advantage is stealth and PHP-specific obfuscation.

name: shellnoob
description: ShellNoob is a shellcode writing toolkit that helps security researchers write, convert, and test shellcode for exploit development. It provides ready-to-use shellcode snippets and conversion utilities between different formats.
how_it_works: ShellNoob provides a set of utilities for shellcode development: assembly-to-opcode conversion, opcode-to-shellcode extraction, format conversion (hex, C array, Python, Perl, Ruby), and shellcode testing. It includes pre-built shellcode for common operations (execve, bind shell, reverse shell) across multiple architectures (x86, x86_64, ARM). The tool automates null byte removal, encoding for bad character avoidance, and provides a testing harness that executes shellcode in a controlled environment.
usage_scenarios: Writing custom shellcode for exploit development. Converting shellcode between formats for different exploit frameworks. Testing shellcode in a safe environment. Learning shellcode development techniques.
strengths: Format conversion between all common types
Pre-built shellcode library for common operations
weaknesses: Requires assembly knowledge for custom shellcode
Pre-built shellcode may be detected by AV/EDR
Less feature-rich than pwntools for exploit development
Not actively maintained
comparison: pwntools provides more comprehensive exploit development tools including shellcraft for shellcode generation. msfvenom (Metasploit) generates shellcode with encoding and encryption. ShellStorm offers an online shellcode database. ShellNoob's advantage is its format conversion utilities.

name: lbd
description: LBD (Load Balancer Detector) is a reconnaissance tool that detects whether a domain is using load balancing and identifies the load balancing technology in use. It uses multiple detection techniques including DNS, HTTP, and timing analysis.
how_it_works: LBD uses three primary detection methods: DNS-based detection (checking if the domain resolves to multiple IP addresses), HTTP-based detection (comparing response headers, content, and cookies across multiple requests for differences that indicate different backend servers), and timing-based detection (measuring response time variations that suggest request routing to different servers). By combining these techniques, it can detect DNS round-robin, application-level load balancing, and hardware load balancers.
usage_scenarios: Reconnaissance to understand target infrastructure. Identifying load balancers before launching attacks. Mapping backend server architecture. Validating load balancer configuration.
weaknesses: Limited output options
No API integration
Can miss sophisticated load balancing configurations
Not actively maintained
comparison: wafw00f focuses on WAF detection rather than load balancers. Halberd is a more comprehensive load balancer detector but is less maintained. LBD provides a quick and focused check for load balancing.

name: wafw00f
description: Wafw00f is a Web Application Firewall (WAF) detection tool that identifies the WAF protecting a web application. Knowing the WAF type helps penetration testers customize their attacks to bypass specific WAF rules and signatures.
how_it_works: Wafw00f sends both normal and malformed HTTP requests to the target and analyzes the responses. Normal requests establish a baseline, while malicious requests (containing SQL injection, XSS, and other attack payloads) trigger the WAF. By analyzing the WAF's response headers, error pages, status codes, and cookie values, Wafw00f can fingerprint the specific WAF product. It maintains a database of known WAF signatures including Cloudflare, ModSecurity, Imperva, F5, and many others.
usage_scenarios: Pre-attack reconnaissance to identify WAF protections. Customizing attack payloads to bypass specific WAF products. Validating WAF deployment and configuration. Security assessments requiring WAF bypass techniques.
strengths: Multiple detection methods (headers, cookies, response content)
weaknesses: Some WAFs actively try to avoid detection
Cannot test for WAF bypass effectiveness
May produce false negatives behind CDN proxies
Limited update frequency for new WAF signatures
comparison: IdentYwaf is a newer alternative with more WAF signatures. Wappalyzer detects some WAFs as part of broader tech fingerprinting. Nmap's http-waf-detect script provides similar detection. Wafw00f remains the most popular dedicated WAF detector.

name: identywaf
description: IdentYwaf is a WAF identification tool that uses blind detection techniques to identify web application firewalls even when they don't reveal themselves through obvious headers or error pages.
how_it_works: IdentYwaf uses a blind detection approach where it sends a series of requests containing known malicious payloads and analyzes how the target responds differently compared to benign requests. It doesn't rely on obvious WAF indicators like 'Server: ModSecurity' headers. Instead, it looks for subtle response differences: blocked request patterns, custom block pages, HTTP status code patterns, and timing differences. The tool maintains a database of WAF-specific behavioral fingerprints.
usage_scenarios: Identifying WAFs that attempt to hide their presence. Detecting CDN-based WAFs like Cloudflare and AWS WAF. Pre-attack reconnaissance when WAFw00f fails to detect. Testing WAF configuration and blocking behavior.
strengths: Blind detection - works even on stealthy WAFs
Larger WAF signature database than wafw00f
weaknesses: Slower than wafw00f due to multiple request rounds
Can trigger WAF alerts or blocking
False positives with aggressive rate limiting
Requires target to actually block malicious requests
comparison: wafw00f is faster but relies more on obvious WAF indicators. Nmap's http-waf-detect is simpler. IdentYwaf's advantage is its blind detection capability for stealthy WAFs.

name: fimap
description: Fimap is a local/remote file inclusion vulnerability scanner and exploitation tool. It can detect and exploit LFI/RFI vulnerabilities in PHP, Python, and other web application technologies, with the ability to automatically find and exploit log poisoning and other LFI-to-RCE techniques.
how_it_works: Fimap scans for file inclusion vulnerabilities by injecting paths to known files (like /etc/passwd) into target parameters. Once LFI is confirmed, it attempts multiple exploitation techniques: log poisoning (injecting PHP code into access logs via User-Agent, then including the log file), /proc/self/environ injection, PHP filter wrappers (php://filter/convert.base64-encode), and PHP input wrappers (php://input). For RFI, it can automatically serve a malicious PHP file from the attacker's machine and trigger its inclusion on the target.
usage_scenarios: Detecting and exploiting local file inclusion vulnerabilities. Escalating LFI to remote code execution through log poisoning. Testing PHP filter and stream wrapper abuse. Automating LFI/RFI testing across large applications.
strengths: Supports PHP, Python, and other technologies
weaknesses: PHP-focused - less effective on other technologies
Log paths may not match target configuration
Antivirus can detect injected payloads
Less actively maintained
comparison: DotDotPwn focuses on directory traversal but not LFI exploitation. Kadimus is an LFI exploitation tool with similar capabilities. Burp Suite can test for LFI manually but doesn't automate exploitation. Fimap's advantage is its automated LFI-to-RCE chain.

name: kadimus
description: Kadimus is a local file inclusion exploitation tool that specializes in converting LFI vulnerabilities into remote code execution. It automates several LFI-to-RCE techniques including log poisoning, /proc/self/environ injection, and PHP filter abuse.
how_it_works: Kadimus works in multiple phases: first it confirms the LFI vulnerability by including known files, then it attempts various RCE escalation techniques. For log poisoning, it identifies the web server's access log path (trying Apache, Nginx, and other common paths), injects PHP code via the User-Agent header in a separate request, then includes the log file through the LFI. It also attempts /proc/self/environ injection, PHP session file inclusion, and PHP filter wrappers for source code disclosure. Successful RCE provides a pseudo-shell for command execution.
usage_scenarios: Exploiting LFI vulnerabilities to gain shell access. Source code disclosure through PHP filter wrappers. Log poisoning for RCE through LFI. Automated LFI exploitation during penetration tests.
weaknesses: PHP-specific exploitation techniques
Log path guessing may fail on custom configurations
No WAF evasion built in
Less comprehensive than Fimap for RFI testing
comparison: Fimap provides similar LFI/RFI exploitation with broader technology support. DotDotPwn tests path traversal but doesn't exploit LFI. Kadimus is more focused on LFI-to-RCE automation specifically.

name: testssl
description: testssl.sh is a comprehensive TLS/SSL security testing tool that checks server configuration for known vulnerabilities (Heartbleed, POODLE, etc.), cipher suite quality, certificate issues, and protocol support. It produces detailed, color-coded output for quick assessment.
how_it_works: testssl.sh performs a battery of tests against the target's TLS implementation. It checks protocol support (SSLv2 through TLS 1.3), cipher suites (ordering, strength, forward secrecy), certificate details (chain, validity, OCSP, CRL), and known vulnerabilities (Heartbleed, POODLE, CCS Injection, Logjam, Freak, DROWN, Beast, Lucky13). Each test uses raw socket connections and TLS handshake manipulation to verify the server's behavior, not just its configuration files. Results are categorized by severity with color coding.
usage_scenarios: Auditing TLS configurations on production servers. Checking for known SSL/TLS vulnerabilities. Validating cipher suite configurations. Compliance testing against PCI-DSS and other standards.
strengths: Most comprehensive TLS/SSL testing tool available
Tests for all major known TLS vulnerabilities
weaknesses: Can be slow on hosts with many cipher suites
Bash-only - not easily scriptable from other languages
Output format not machine-readable by default (use --json)
Rate limiting can cause test failures on aggressive hosts
comparison: sslscan provides similar cipher enumeration but less vulnerability testing. sslyze is a Python alternative with better programmatic output. nmap's ssl-enum-ciphers script covers basic cipher checking. testssl.sh is the most comprehensive standalone TLS testing tool.

name: sslscan
description: SSLScan queries SSL/TLS services and reports the protocol versions, cipher suites, key exchange algorithms, and certificate details. It's a fast, lightweight tool for quickly assessing TLS configuration.
how_it_works: SSLScan connects to the target service and performs TLS handshakes with every supported cipher suite and protocol version combination. By systematically attempting each combination, it builds a complete picture of what the server supports. It checks SSLv2, SSLv3, TLS 1.0 through 1.3, and for each protocol version, tests all cipher suites. The tool also examines the certificate chain, checking for validity, expiration, and CA information. Results show preferred and supported ciphers with their key strengths.
usage_scenarios: Quick assessment of TLS configuration. Identifying weak cipher suites and deprecated protocols. Certificate chain validation. Pre-penetration test reconnaissance of SSL services.
weaknesses: No vulnerability testing (unlike testssl.sh)
Limited to cipher/protocol enumeration
Cannot test for Heartbleed, POODLE, etc.
Less detailed output than testssl.sh
comparison: testssl.sh is far more comprehensive with vulnerability testing. sslyze provides similar functionality with Python scripting. nmap's ssl-enum-ciphers is slower but integrates with Nmap workflows. SSLScan's advantage is speed and simplicity.

name: sslyze
description: SSLyze is a Python library and CLI tool for analyzing the SSL/TLS configuration of servers. It provides fast, comprehensive scanning with structured output suitable for automation and CI/CD integration.
how_it_works: SSLyze uses Python's SSL/TLS libraries to connect to target servers and perform comprehensive configuration analysis. It supports OpenSSl and Python's ssl module as backends, allowing it to test every supported cipher suite and protocol version. Each scan plugin handles a specific aspect: certificate validation, cipher suite analysis, protocol support, compression, session renegotiation, and known vulnerability checks (Heartbleed, OpenSSL CCS Injection, ROBOT). Results are available in JSON format for programmatic processing.
usage_scenarios: Automated TLS configuration monitoring in CI/CD pipelines. Bulk scanning of TLS services across an organization. Compliance verification for TLS security standards. Integration with security dashboards and reporting tools.
strengths: JSON output for automation and dashboards
weaknesses: Less comprehensive vulnerability testing than testssl.sh
Python dependency management
Some features require specific OpenSSL versions
Documentation could be more thorough
comparison: testssl.sh is more comprehensive for manual testing. sslscan is faster for simple cipher enumeration. SSLyze's advantage is its Python API for automation and CI/CD integration.

name: webshells
description: WebShells is a collection of common PHP, ASP, ASPX, JSP, and other web shell scripts packaged for penetration testers. The collection includes simple command execution shells, file managers, and full-featured web shells with database access and privilege escalation capabilities.
how_it_works: This is a collection rather than a tool - it provides ready-to-use web shell scripts in multiple languages. PHP shells include simple one-liners (<?php system($_GET['cmd']); ?>), file managers (WSO, b374k), and feature-rich shells (Alfa Shell). ASP/ASPX shells provide similar functionality on Windows/IIS servers. JSP shells target Java application servers. Each shell provides different capabilities from basic command execution to full file management, database access, and network tunneling.
usage_scenarios: Post-exploitation persistence through web shells. File management on compromised web servers. Database access through web-based interfaces. Network pivoting through web application servers.
commands: python3 -m http.server 8000  # Serve shells for upload
weaknesses: Most shells are well-known to AV/EDR solutions
No encryption or obfuscation by default
Uploading shells requires another vulnerability
Ethical considerations - easily abused
comparison: Weevely generates obfuscated PHP shells that evade detection. Metasploit provides payload generation with encoding. Custom shells avoid signature detection. This collection provides variety but at the cost of detectability.

name: brutex
description: BruteX is an automated brute-forcing tool that attacks all running services on a target. It automatically discovers open ports and services, then launches appropriate brute-force attacks against each one using default credentials.
how_it_works: BruteX first performs a port scan (using Nmap) to identify running services on the target. For each discovered service, it selects the appropriate Hydra module and launches a brute-force attack using built-in username and password lists. It supports SSH, FTP, HTTP form-based authentication, SMTP, SNMP, and many other protocols. The automation means a single command can test multiple services for default or weak credentials without manual service-by-service configuration.
usage_scenarios: Automated credential testing across all services on a target. Default credential checking after initial port scanning. Quick assessment of authentication security. Penetration testing with limited time for manual brute-forcing.
strengths: Automated - discovers and attacks all services
Uses Hydra as backend for reliable protocol support
weaknesses: Very noisy - will trigger IDS/IPS alerts
No rate limiting - can cause account lockouts
Built-in wordlists are small
Limited customization of attack parameters
comparison: Hydra provides more control over individual service attacks. Ncrack is faster for network service brute-forcing. Medusa supports parallel attacks on multiple hosts. BruteX's advantage is automation across all discovered services.

name: feroxbuster
description: Feroxbuster is a recursive content discovery tool written in Rust that combines the speed of Gobuster with recursive directory scanning and automatic wildcard detection. It's designed to be fast, simple, and effective at discovering hidden content on web servers.
how_it_works: Feroxbuster uses Rust's async/await model with Tokio for concurrent HTTP requests. It starts by scanning a base directory with a wordlist, and for each discovered directory, automatically spawns a new scanning task (recursive mode). It performs automatic wildcard detection by sending random strings and analyzing responses - if every random string returns 200, it identifies the wildcard pattern and filters those responses. The tool supports response filtering by status code, word count, line count, and size, and can extract links from response bodies for additional discovery.
usage_scenarios: Comprehensive recursive directory discovery. Finding hidden content that non-recursive tools miss. Scanning with automatic wildcard and custom 404 detection. Large-scale content discovery with filtering.
strengths: Recursive scanning - discovers nested directories automatically
Wildcard detection filters out custom 404 pages
weaknesses: Can generate enormous amounts of traffic with deep recursion
Memory usage grows with recursive depth
No DNS or vhost scanning modes
Requires careful tuning to avoid overwhelming targets
comparison: Gobuster is faster for flat directory scanning but lacks recursion. ffuf is similarly fast with more fuzzing modes but no auto-recursion. DIRB is much slower and single-threaded. Feroxbuster's killer feature is recursive scanning with wildcard detection.

name: rustscan
description: RustScan is a modern port scanner that uses Rust's async I/O to achieve scanning speeds 10x faster than Nmap. It can scan all 65,535 ports in under 5 seconds, then pipes results to Nmap for detailed service enumeration.
how_it_works: RustScan uses Rust's asynchronous networking to send SYN packets at extremely high rates. It divides the port range into batches and processes them concurrently, utilizing all available CPU cores. Unlike Nmap's sequential scanning, RustScan can probe thousands of ports simultaneously. Once open ports are identified, it can automatically invoke Nmap with service-specific scripts against only the open ports, combining RustScan's speed with Nmap's deep enumeration capabilities.
usage_scenarios: Rapid port discovery during time-sensitive engagements. Pre-Nmap scanning to quickly identify open ports. Large-scale network reconnaissance. Initial footprinting when speed is critical.
strengths: 10x faster than Nmap for port discovery
Seamless Nmap integration for deep scanning
Supports CIDR ranges and target lists
weaknesses: Only does port scanning - no service detection on its own
Can overwhelm firewalls and trigger IDS
Requires ulimit adjustment for full speed
Less stealthy than Nmap's slower scan modes
comparison: Masscan is similarly fast but less accurate and doesn't integrate with Nmap. Nmap is slower but provides comprehensive service detection. RustScan's advantage is its speed combined with automatic Nmap integration.

name: xsstrike
description: XSStrike is an advanced XSS detection and exploitation tool that uses intelligent payload generation based on response analysis. Unlike other XSS scanners that use static payload lists, XSStrike analyzes the response context and generates payloads specifically crafted for the injection point.
how_it_works: XSStrike first sends a probe request to analyze how the target reflects input. It identifies the injection context (HTML attribute, JavaScript string, CSS, etc.) and the characters that are filtered or escaped. Based on this analysis, it generates context-aware payloads that are more likely to bypass filters. It includes a fuzzing engine that tests for DOM-based XSS by analyzing JavaScript source code for dangerous sinks (innerHTML, document.write, eval). The tool also performs HTML response crawling to find additional injection points.
usage_scenarios: Targeted XSS testing with intelligent payload generation. Bypassing WAF filters through context-aware payloads. DOM-based XSS detection. Automated XSS scanning with lower false positive rates.
strengths: DOM XSS detection through JavaScript analysis
Crawler for discovering additional injection points
Lower false positive rate than static payload tools
weaknesses: Slower than brute-force XSS tools
No GUI - command line only
Python dependencies can be tricky
DOM analysis is not 100% reliable
comparison: Dalfox is faster and has better output formatting. XSSer uses static payloads but with more encoding options. OWASP ZAP includes XSS testing as part of its scanner. XSStrike's advantage is its intelligent payload generation.

name: dalfox
description: Dalfox is a fast XSS scanner and parameter analysis tool written in Go. It combines fast scanning with intelligent payload generation, DOM analysis, and comprehensive reporting for efficient XSS testing.
how_it_works: Dalfox uses Go's concurrency for fast parallel scanning. It first analyzes the target's parameter handling (reflection points, encoding behavior, filter characteristics), then selects appropriate payloads from its extensive database. It supports reflected XSS, stored XSS detection, and DOM-based XSS through static JavaScript analysis. The tool includes built-in encoding and obfuscation techniques for WAF bypass, and supports headless browser-based verification for DOM XSS. Results are output in plain text or JSON for integration.
usage_scenarios: Fast XSS scanning across large applications. Parameter analysis and reflection testing. Automated XSS testing in CI/CD pipelines. Bulk scanning of multiple targets.
strengths: Blind XSS testing with callback server
weaknesses: Less intelligent payload generation than XSStrike
DOM XSS detection is limited without headless browser
Go binary - less extensible than Python tools
Some payloads may trigger WAF blocks
comparison: XSStrike has smarter payload generation but is slower. XSSer has more encoding options but less modern architecture. Dalfox's advantage is speed and blind XSS support.

name: nuclei
description: Nuclei is a fast, template-based vulnerability scanner that uses YAML templates to define vulnerability checks. With thousands of community-contributed templates, it can detect a vast range of vulnerabilities, misconfigurations, and exposed services across web applications, networks, and cloud infrastructure.
how_it_works: Nuclei processes YAML templates that define how to send requests and match responses. Each template specifies one or more HTTP/DNS/TCP requests and matching conditions (status code, body regex, header presence, DSL expressions). The engine processes templates concurrently, sending requests to targets and matching responses against template conditions. Templates can be chained for multi-step vulnerability verification. The template format supports variables, expressions, and conditional logic for complex detection scenarios. Templates are categorized by severity, tag, and author.
usage_scenarios: Rapid vulnerability scanning with community templates. Custom vulnerability scanning with organization-specific templates. Bug bounty hunting across large attack surfaces. Continuous security monitoring in CI/CD pipelines.
strengths: 7,000+ community templates covering thousands of CVEs
YAML templates easy to write and customize
Multi-protocol support (HTTP, DNS, TCP, SSL, WebSocket)
weaknesses: Template quality varies - some have high false positive rates
Requires regular template updates
Can be noisy and trigger rate limiting
Complex templates can be difficult to debug
comparison: Nikto uses hardcoded checks while Nuclei uses extensible templates. OWASP ZAP provides deeper scanning but is slower. Metasploit's auxiliary modules offer similar template-based scanning. Nuclei's advantage is its template ecosystem and speed.

name: rest-shell
description: REST-Shell is a tool for interacting with RESTful APIs from the command line. It provides a shell-like interface for making HTTP requests, inspecting responses, and automating API testing workflows.
how_it_works: REST-Shell provides an interactive shell where users can construct and send HTTP requests to RESTful APIs. It maintains session state (authentication tokens, cookies, headers) across requests. The shell supports environment variables for parameterization, command history, and output piping. It can format JSON responses for readability and supports HTTP methods (GET, POST, PUT, DELETE, PATCH) with custom headers and body content.
usage_scenarios: Interactive API testing and exploration. Automated API security testing scripts. Debugging RESTful services. Penetration testing of API-based applications.
weaknesses: Less feature-rich than Postman or Insomnia
Limited to RESTful APIs
No built-in vulnerability testing
Small community and documentation
comparison: Postman provides a full-featured GUI for API testing. HTTPie is a simpler command-line alternative. curl is more universal but less interactive. REST-Shell provides a middle ground with session management.

name: jwt_tool
description: JWT_Tool is a toolkit for testing JSON Web Token (JWT) implementations. It can decode, forge, and attack JWTs, testing for common implementation flaws like algorithm none, key confusion, weak secrets, and signature bypass.
how_it_works: JWT_Tool parses JWTs into their header, payload, and signature components. It tests for multiple attack vectors: the 'none' algorithm attack (removing the signature), algorithm confusion (switching RS256 to HS256 and using the public key as HMAC secret), weak secret brute-forcing (using wordlists against HS256 tokens), JWK injection (embedding a public key in the header), and JKU/X5U header injection (pointing to attacker-controlled key URLs). For each attack, it generates a modified JWT that can be tested against the target application.
usage_scenarios: Testing JWT authentication implementations for security flaws. Forging tokens to escalate privileges or impersonate users. Brute-forcing weak JWT signing secrets. Validating JWT security configurations.
strengths: Automatic testing of all known JWT vulnerabilities
weaknesses: JWT-specific only - doesn't test other auth mechanisms
Success depends on server's JWT implementation flaws
No automated testing against live targets
Brute-force can be slow for complex secrets
comparison: hashcat can crack JWT secrets faster. Burp Suite's JSON Web Token plugin provides GUI-based JWT testing. jwt-cracker focuses specifically on brute-forcing. JWT_Tool's advantage is its comprehensive attack coverage.

name: nosqlmap
description: NoSQLMap is an automated tool for auditing and exploiting NoSQL databases and web applications that use them. It specifically targets MongoDB, CouchDB, and other NoSQL databases that traditional SQL injection tools cannot test.
how_it_works: NoSQLMap exploits the fundamental differences between SQL and NoSQL databases. Instead of SQL injection, it uses NoSQL-specific injection techniques: MongoDB operator injection ($gt, $ne, $regex operators to bypass authentication), JavaScript injection (passing JavaScript code to $where clauses), and BSON payload injection. For CouchDB, it exploits MapReduce functions and design documents. The tool can also extract data from unauthenticated MongoDB instances (a common misconfiguration), enumerate databases and collections, and clone data.
usage_scenarios: Testing applications that use MongoDB, CouchDB, or other NoSQL databases. Exploiting NoSQL injection to bypass authentication. Extracting data from misconfigured NoSQL instances. Demonstrating the security implications of NoSQL query construction.
strengths: Only comprehensive tool for NoSQL injection testing
Can exploit both database-level and application-level vulnerabilities
weaknesses: Less mature than SQLMap
Limited to MongoDB and CouchDB primarily
Python 2 codebase
No support for Redis, DynamoDB, or Cassandra injection
comparison: SQLMap is the equivalent for SQL databases but doesn't understand NoSQL syntax. MongoAudit focuses specifically on MongoDB security auditing. NoSQLMap is the most comprehensive NoSQL-specific attack tool.

name: silver
description: Silver is a PoC exploit generator for CVEs. It automates the creation of proof-of-concept exploits from vulnerability descriptions, making it easier to verify whether a specific CVE affects a target system.
how_it_works: Silver takes a CVE identifier or vulnerability description and generates a PoC exploit script. It uses vulnerability database information (CVE details, exploit-db references) to construct a basic exploit framework that targets the specific vulnerability. The generated script includes the necessary network/protocol handling, payload delivery, and success verification. Users can customize the generated exploit for their specific target environment.
usage_scenarios: Quickly generating PoC exploits for vulnerability verification. Testing whether systems are vulnerable to specific CVEs. Automating vulnerability verification across multiple targets. Learning exploit development from generated examples.
strengths: Rapid PoC generation from CVE identifiers
weaknesses: Generated exploits are basic - may need significant customization
Not all CVEs have templates
Quality depends on available vulnerability information
Ethical concerns about automated exploit generation
comparison: Metasploit provides more mature and tested exploits but for fewer CVEs. ExploitDB offers manual exploit code. Nuclei templates provide non-exploitation verification. Silver's advantage is rapid PoC generation for verification.

name: alfabeto
description: Alfabeto is a DNS zone transfer and enumeration tool designed for web reconnaissance. It automates DNS queries to extract subdomain information and identify DNS misconfigurations.
how_it_works: Alfabeto performs DNS zone transfer attempts against target nameservers using AXFR queries. If zone transfers are permitted (a common misconfiguration), it retrieves the complete DNS record set including all subdomains, mail servers, and internal hostnames. It also supports brute-force subdomain enumeration by iterating through wordlists and checking DNS resolution.
usage_scenarios: DNS reconnaissance during penetration tests. Discovering internal hostnames through zone transfer misconfiguration. Subdomain enumeration for attack surface mapping. Identifying DNS security weaknesses.
weaknesses: Zone transfers are increasingly rare due to better DNS security
Single-threaded enumeration is slow
Limited output formats
comparison: Sublist3r and Amass provide more comprehensive subdomain enumeration. DNSrecon is more feature-rich for DNS testing. Alfabeto is simpler and more focused.

name: apache-users
description: Apache-users is a tool that enumerates users on Apache web servers by exploiting the mod_userdir module. When this module is enabled, it exposes user directories at /~username/ URLs.
how_it_works: The tool sends HTTP requests to /~username/ paths for each entry in a username wordlist. When mod_userdir is enabled, valid usernames return a 200 or 403 response (indicating the user exists), while invalid usernames return 404. By iterating through common usernames, it builds a list of valid system users on the Apache server.
usage_scenarios: User enumeration on Apache servers with mod_userdir enabled. Discovering valid usernames for brute-force attacks. Reconnaissance before targeted authentication attacks.
strengths: Simple and effective when mod_userdir is enabled
weaknesses: Only works when mod_userdir is enabled
Apache-specific
Limited to username discovery - no further exploitation
comparison: Hydra can brute-force authentication with discovered usernames. Nmap's http-userdir-enum script provides similar functionality. This tool is more focused and simpler.

name: arjun
description: Arjun is an HTTP parameter discovery tool that finds hidden HTTP parameters that other scanners miss. It uses intelligent detection heuristics to identify valid parameters even when responses are subtle.
how_it_works: Arjun sends requests with different parameter names and compares responses to a baseline request. Unlike simple parameter fuzzing that only checks status codes, Arjun uses multiple detection methods: response content comparison (diff-based), response time analysis, response length comparison, and heuristic matching. It handles custom 404 pages, rate limiting, and pagination parameters intelligently. The tool uses smart parameter guessing based on common naming patterns and API conventions.
usage_scenarios: Discovering hidden API parameters during web application testing. Finding undocumented API endpoints. Parameter discovery for bug bounty hunting. Identifying debug or admin parameters.
strengths: Intelligent parameter detection beyond simple status codes
weaknesses: Can miss parameters that don't affect response content
Rate limiting can slow discovery
No authentication testing for protected endpoints
Python dependencies
comparison: Wfuzz can fuzz parameters but is less intelligent about detection. ffuf can fuzz parameters but requires manual analysis. ParamSpider discovers parameters from cached URLs. Arjun's advantage is its intelligent detection heuristics.

name: bucket-stream
description: Bucket Stream is a tool that monitors Amazon S3 buckets for changes and publicly accessible data. It discovers open S3 buckets by permuting common bucket names and checking for public access permissions.
how_it_works: Bucket Stream generates permutations of common bucket naming patterns (company name, company-backup, company-logs, etc.) and checks each for public accessibility. When a publicly accessible bucket is found, it enumerates the bucket contents and can monitor for changes over time. It uses the AWS S3 API without authentication to test for anonymous access permissions.
usage_scenarios: Discovering exposed S3 buckets during cloud security assessments. Monitoring for data leakage through misconfigured S3 permissions. Cloud reconnaissance for organizations using AWS.
weaknesses: AWS-specific - only tests S3 buckets
Rate limiting by AWS API
Bucket naming permutations can be hit-or-miss
No testing for authenticated bucket access
comparison: S3Scanner provides similar functionality with more exhaustive checking. AWSBucketDump focuses on dumping bucket contents. CloudSploit provides broader AWS security testing. Bucket Stream is focused on discovery and monitoring.

name: burpcollaborator-docker
description: A Docker-based alternative to Burp Suite's Collaborator server. It provides out-of-band interaction detection for blind vulnerability testing without requiring a Burp Suite Professional license.
how_it_works: The tool deploys a self-hosted Collaborator-like server using Docker containers. It provides DNS, HTTP, and HTTPS interaction logging that can be used to detect blind injection vulnerabilities. When testing for blind XSS, SSRF, or other out-of-band vulnerabilities, the tester uses the self-hosted domain in payloads. The server logs all interactions (DNS lookups, HTTP requests) that result from successful injection on the target.
usage_scenarios: Out-of-band vulnerability testing without Burp Suite Professional. Self-hosted alternative for blind injection testing. Blind XSS and SSRF detection in environments where external callback servers are blocked.
commands: Configure your testing tools to use the collaborator domain
strengths: Supports DNS, HTTP, HTTPS interaction logging
weaknesses: Requires Docker infrastructure
Requires a registered domain name
No integration with Burp Suite workflow
Manual correlation of interactions with payloads
comparison: Burp Collaborator is integrated with Burp Suite Professional. interact.sh is ProjectDiscovery's free alternative with better tooling. This Docker solution provides a self-hosted option for maximum control.

name: cloudflare-scraper
description: Cloudflare-scraper is a tool that bypasses Cloudflare's anti-bot protection to access protected websites. It handles the JavaScript challenge that Cloudflare presents to automated tools and browsers.
how_it_works: The tool uses a headless browser or Python requests with challenge-solving logic to bypass Cloudflare's JavaScript challenge page. When Cloudflare detects an automated request, it serves a challenge page requiring JavaScript execution. This tool automatically solves the challenge by executing the JavaScript, extracting the clearance cookie, and using it for subsequent requests. It handles Cloudflare's various protection levels including JS challenge, CAPTCHA challenge, and browser integrity checks.
usage_scenarios: Accessing websites protected by Cloudflare during penetration tests. Bypassing anti-bot protection for automated scanning. Web scraping of Cloudflare-protected targets. Testing the effectiveness of Cloudflare's bot detection.
strengths: Python library for integration with other tools
weaknesses: Cannot bypass CAPTCHA challenges
Cloudflare frequently updates their protection
Requires Node.js for challenge solving
Detection evasion is a cat-and-mouse game
comparison: FlareSolverr provides a proxy-based approach to Cloudflare bypass. CloudScraper is a similar Python library. Selenium with undetected-chromedriver handles more complex challenges. This tool is simpler but less effective against newer protections.

name: cloudfail
description: CloudFail is a reconnaissance tool that identifies the real IP address of websites behind Cloudflare's CDN protection. It uses multiple techniques to uncover origin servers that Cloudflare is meant to hide.
how_it_works: CloudFail uses three main techniques: DNS history lookup (checking historical DNS records from before Cloudflare was configured), subdomain resolution (finding subdomains that don't use Cloudflare), and CrimeDNS resolution (using crime-finding databases to locate historical IP associations). By combining these data sources, it can often find the origin IP address that Cloudflare's proxy is meant to protect.
usage_scenarios: Uncovering origin IP addresses behind Cloudflare for direct targeting. Testing the effectiveness of Cloudflare's IP protection. Reconnaissance before penetration testing Cloudflare-protected sites.
weaknesses: Not always effective - depends on target's DNS configuration history
Historical DNS data may be outdated
Cloudflare improves protection regularly
Requires external data sources
comparison: CrimeIP provides similar historical DNS lookup. Censys can find origin IPs through certificate data. Shodan can discover exposed services. CloudFail combines multiple techniques in one tool.

name: crackstation
