description: CrackStation's password cracking tools and dictionaries. Includes massive wordlists and lookup tables for offline password hash cracking using precomputed tables.
how_it_works: The CrackStation approach uses precomputed hash lookup tables rather than brute-force computation. The included wordlists contain billions of real-world passwords from data breaches, sorted by frequency. When cracking a hash, the tool looks it up in the precomputed table for instant results, rather than computing hashes on the fly. This is dramatically faster than traditional brute-force for common passwords.
usage_scenarios: Offline password hash cracking. Testing password strength against real-world breach data. Demonstrating the weakness of common passwords. Security awareness training.
strengths: Precomputed lookup tables for instant cracking
Real breach data - passwords people actually use
weaknesses: Huge download size (15GB+ for full set)
Cannot crack salted hashes with lookup tables
Ethical concerns with breach data
Only covers common passwords in the dataset
comparison: RockYou wordlist is smaller but most commonly used. SecLists provides curated wordlists for different scenarios. HaveIBeenPwned offers password hash checking without distributing plaintext. CrackStation's advantage is its massive size.

name: dirstalk
description: Dirstalk is a multi-threaded directory discovery tool inspired by DirBuster, written in Go. It uses sitemap-based scanning and supports custom dictionary files for discovering hidden paths on web servers.
how_it_works: Dirstalk uses Go's concurrency model for fast multi-threaded scanning. It can use standard wordlists or parse existing sitemap.xml files to build targeted dictionaries. The tool supports HTTP/HTTPS, configurable threads, request throttling, and result filtering by status code. It maintains a persistent connection pool for efficiency and can resume interrupted scans.
usage_scenarios: Directory discovery with sitemap-informed wordlists. Fast multi-threaded content discovery. Scanning with custom dictionaries for targeted testing.
weaknesses: Less well-known than ffuf or Gobuster
Smaller community and fewer features
No recursive scanning mode
Limited output format options
comparison: ffuf and Gobuster are more popular and feature-rich. Feroxbuster provides recursive scanning. DirBuster (Java) is the original but is slow and deprecated. Dirstalk's sitemap feature is unique.

name: dontgo403
description: Dontgo403 is a tool that attempts to bypass 403 (Forbidden) pages using various HTTP request manipulation techniques. It tries multiple bypass methods to access restricted resources.
how_it_works: Dontgo403 sends multiple variations of the original request with modifications designed to bypass access controls: URL path manipulation (adding /, %2f, /.), HTTP method changes (GET to POST, PUT, OPTIONS, HEAD), header modifications (X-Forwarded-For, X-Original-URL, X-Rewrite-URL), and URL encoding variations. For each variation, it checks if the response changes from 403 to 200 or another success code, indicating the access control was bypassed.
usage_scenarios: Bypassing 403 restrictions on web application paths. Testing access control implementations. Finding hidden admin panels and restricted directories. Demonstrating access control misconfigurations.
strengths: Automated 403 bypass testing with multiple techniques
Proxy support for routing through tools
weaknesses: Many bypass attempts may not work on properly configured servers
Can trigger rate limiting or WAF blocks
Limited to common bypass techniques
No exploitation beyond access bypass
comparison: Burp Suite's Autorize extension tests authorization bypass. Manual testing with curl can achieve the same results. This tool automates the common bypass techniques for efficiency.

name: fuxploider
description: Fuxploider is an automated file upload vulnerability scanner and exploitation tool. It detects and exploits file upload forms by testing various file types and extensions to find bypass opportunities.
how_it_works: Fuxploider identifies file upload forms on web pages, then systematically tests them with different file types and extensions. It tries techniques like double extensions (shell.php.jpg), null byte injection (shell.php%00.jpg), content-type manipulation (changing MIME type), and magic byte injection (adding image file headers to PHP files). When an upload succeeds and is accessible, it confirms the vulnerability. The tool uses a headless browser for JavaScript-heavy upload forms.
usage_scenarios: Testing file upload functionality for security vulnerabilities. Bypassing file type restrictions to upload web shells. Validating upload security controls. Demonstrating the impact of insecure file upload handling.
strengths: Automated file upload testing with bypass techniques
weaknesses: Python dependencies can be complex
Not all bypass techniques work on modern frameworks
Headless browser can be slow
May trigger server-side security controls
comparison: Manual Burp Suite testing provides more control. Weevely generates uploadable PHP shells. Fuxploider automates the discovery and bypass testing process.

name: gobuster-dir
description: Gobuster directory mode specifically targets web directory and file discovery. This is the directory-focused variant of the Gobuster tool with optimizations for web content enumeration.
how_it_works: Same as Gobuster but focused on directory enumeration with optimizations for web-specific patterns. It sends concurrent HTTP requests for each wordlist entry appended to the target URL, supporting custom extensions, status code filtering, and pattern-based discovery.
usage_scenarios: Directory and file enumeration on web servers. Finding hidden content and backup files. Content discovery during web application testing.
weaknesses: Same as Gobuster core weaknesses
comparison: Same as Gobuster main entry.

name: hakrawler
description: Crawls web applications to discover URLs, API endpoints, and JavaScript files by parsing HTML for links, extracting URLs from JavaScript source code, and reading robots.txt/sitemap.xml. Its Go-based concurrency model enables rapid crawling of large applications while respecting scope boundaries.

name: httprecon
description: A web server fingerprinting tool that identifies server software and versions by analyzing HTTP response patterns including header ordering, status line formatting, and error page content. It compares observed patterns against a database of known server signatures.

name: hydra-web
description: A web-specific variant of the Hydra brute-force tool that tests HTTP form-based authentication, web login pages, and API endpoints. It sends POST requests with username/password combinations from wordlists, analyzing response content and status codes to identify successful logins on web applications.

name: interactsh
description: Detects blind vulnerabilities (SSRF, XSS, SQLi, RCE) by generating unique callback subdomains that, when triggered by a vulnerable server, confirm the injection was successful. The tool runs an interaction server that logs DNS lookups, HTTP requests, and SMTP connections to the generated subdomains, providing proof of exploitation for blind injection points.

name: joomlafinder
description: A Joomla CMS discovery tool that identifies Joomla installations on target domains by checking for characteristic files, directories, and metadata. It fingerprints the Joomla version and identifies installed extensions for targeted vulnerability assessment.

name: jsparser
description: A JavaScript parser and analyzer for security researchers that extracts URLs, API endpoints, secrets, and interesting patterns from JavaScript files. It helps identify hidden functionality and security-sensitive code in client-side JavaScript.

name: katana
description: Crawls web applications using both traditional HTML parsing and headless browser rendering to discover URLs and endpoints in both static and JavaScript-heavy applications. It supports scope filtering, custom headers, rate limiting, and outputs structured results compatible with other ProjectDiscovery tools.

name: liffy
description: A Local/Remote File Inclusion (LFI/RFI) exploitation tool that automates the process of exploiting file inclusion vulnerabilities to read sensitive files or execute remote code. It supports PHP wrappers, log poisoning, and other LFI-to-RCE techniques.

name: metoscan
description: A web application vulnerability scanner that combines directory discovery, parameter testing, and vulnerability detection into an automated workflow. It tests for common web vulnerabilities including SQL injection, XSS, and file inclusion across discovered application paths.

name: mitmproxy
description: Acts as an intercepting proxy that captures and allows modification of HTTP/S traffic between client and server. It decrypts SSL/TLS traffic using a custom CA certificate, presents requests in an interactive terminal UI for real-time inspection and modification, and supports Python scripting for automated request/response manipulation.

name: morfeus
description: An automated vulnerability scanner that tests web applications for common security issues including SQL injection, XSS, and CSRF. It provides a simplified interface for rapid vulnerability assessment with minimal configuration required.

name: nbtscan
description: A web application security scanning tool that automates the discovery and assessment of target systems. It probes for open services, configurations, and potential vulnerabilities by sending crafted requests and analyzing responses, HTTP request manipulation, parameter fuzzing, payload injection, and response analysis. Results are compiled into actionable reports for security teams to prioritize remediation.

name: nessus-rest
description: A REST API client for the Nessus vulnerability scanner that automates scan management, result retrieval, and report generation. It enables programmatic interaction with Nessus for integration into automated security testing pipelines.

name: nikto2
description: An enhanced version of the Nikto web server scanner with additional test modules, improved performance, and better false positive handling. It checks for dangerous files, outdated software, and misconfigurations using an expanded signature database.

name: nosqlattack
description: A NoSQL injection exploitation tool targeting MongoDB-based web applications. It automates the detection and exploitation of NoSQL injection vulnerabilities through JavaScript injection, operator manipulation, and data extraction techniques specific to document databases.

name: openvas-cli
description: A command-line interface for the OpenVAS vulnerability scanner that automates scan creation, target configuration, and report retrieval. It enables scripted vulnerability assessment workflows without requiring the Greenbone web interface.

name: owasp-zap-cli
description: A command-line interface for OWASP ZAP that enables automated web application scanning from terminal sessions and CI/CD pipelines. It provides access to ZAP's scanning, spidering, and proxy capabilities without launching the GUI.

name: pstrip
description: A HTTP stripping tool that tests for protocol downgrade vulnerabilities by attempting to strip SSL/TLS from HTTPS connections, forcing communication over plaintext HTTP. It demonstrates the risk of HSTS misconfiguration and missing redirect enforcement.

name: pyfiscan
description: A Python-based web application vulnerability scanner that checks for outdated software versions, known vulnerabilities, and misconfigurations. It supports remote version detection for popular web applications and CMS platforms.

name: rcat
description: A netcat-like utility enhanced with SSL/TLS support, port forwarding, and encryption capabilities. It provides a secure alternative to netcat for network operations during penetration tests where traffic encryption is required.

name: retire-js
description: A JavaScript library vulnerability scanner that detects known vulnerabilities in third-party JavaScript libraries used by web applications. It identifies library versions from file contents and checks them against a vulnerability database.

name: risten
description: A web application reconnaissance tool that gathers information about target websites including technologies, server configurations, and security headers. It provides a quick overview of the target's security posture before deeper testing.

name: s3scanner
description: An Amazon S3 bucket security scanner that tests for misconfigured permissions allowing public access to stored data. It enumerates bucket contents and identifies buckets that permit anonymous read or write operations.

name: screenshotter
description: A web screenshot tool that captures visual snapshots of web pages for reconnaissance and documentation. It renders pages using a headless browser and saves screenshots for review, helping analysts quickly assess the nature and purpose of discovered web services.

name: secscan
description: A security scanning tool that combines port scanning, service detection, and vulnerability checking into a unified workflow. It automates the initial reconnaissance phase of penetration tests by discovering and fingerprinting target services.

name: shcheck
description: A security header checker that analyzes HTTP response headers for missing or misconfigured security controls. It tests for the presence and correctness of headers including Content-Security-Policy, X-Frame-Options, HSTS, and X-Content-Type-Options.

name: sites-cli
description: A command-line tool for mass website analysis that processes lists of URLs and extracts metadata, technologies, and security configurations. It enables batch reconnaissance across large numbers of web assets.

name: snallygaster
description: A tool for scanning web servers for sensitive files that should not be publicly accessible, including configuration files, backup archives, database dumps, and log files. It checks for common file paths that expose sensitive information.

name: squid-purge
description: A Squid proxy cache manipulation tool that purges specific URLs from the proxy cache to test for cache poisoning vulnerabilities. It can force the proxy to re-fetch content, enabling testing of cache-based attack scenarios.

name: sslstrip
description: A man-in-the-middle tool that transparently hijacks HTTP traffic and redirects HTTPS requests to HTTP, stripping SSL encryption from the victim's connection. It exploits the user's tendency to not verify HTTPS to intercept credentials transmitted in cleartext.

name: subjack
description: A subdomain takeover tool that tests whether dangling DNS records pointing to decommissioned cloud services can be claimed by attackers. It checks for takeover opportunities across multiple cloud providers including AWS, Azure, and GitHub Pages.

name: surf
description: A lightweight web reconnaissance tool for quickly gathering information about web targets including headers, certificates, and technology stacks. It provides a streamlined interface for initial target assessment.

name: swagger-sp
description: A Swagger/OpenAPI specification security testing tool that analyzes API documentation for vulnerabilities including authentication bypass, excessive permissions, and sensitive data exposure. It parses Swagger JSON to identify attack surfaces in REST APIs.

name: wafninja
description: A WAF bypass testing tool that generates encoded and obfuscated payloads designed to evade Web Application Firewall detection. It applies multiple encoding layers and obfuscation techniques to test WAF effectiveness against modified attack payloads.

name: wafp
description: A WAF profiling tool that identifies the specific Web Application Firewall product protecting a target by analyzing response patterns to crafted requests. It uses signature matching to fingerprint WAF vendors and versions.

name: webanalyze
description: A web technology fingerprinting tool that identifies the CMS, frameworks, web servers, and analytics tools used by a target website. It matches response patterns against a technology signature database similar to Wappalyzer.

name: webdav-scanner
description: A WebDAV protocol scanner that discovers and enumerates WebDAV-enabled directories on web servers. It tests for unauthorized access to WebDAV shares, misconfigured permissions, and sensitive file exposure through the WebDAV protocol.

name: webshag
description: A multi-threaded web application auditing tool that combines directory enumeration, port scanning, and vulnerability checking. It provides a unified reconnaissance and assessment workflow for web application security testing.

name: weevely3
description: An updated version of the Weevely PHP web shell generator that creates stealthy, obfuscated backdoors for maintaining access to compromised web servers. It generates PHP payloads that evade antivirus detection and provide a feature-rich remote shell interface.

name: whatweb-plus
description: An enhanced version of WhatWeb with additional plugin signatures and improved technology detection. It identifies web technologies including CMS platforms, JavaScript libraries, web servers, and analytics tools with greater accuracy and coverage.

name: wordpress-scanner
description: A specialized WordPress security scanner that enumerates plugins, themes, users, and vulnerabilities in WordPress installations. It checks for known vulnerable components and misconfigurations specific to the WordPress platform.

name: wslock
description: A WebSocket security testing tool that analyzes WebSocket connections for authentication bypass, injection vulnerabilities, and data exposure. It intercepts and manipulates WebSocket frames to test the security of real-time communication channels.

name: xsser2
description: An updated version of the XSSer cross-site scripting testing framework with improved payload generation and WAF bypass capabilities. It automates XSS vulnerability detection across multiple injection contexts with enhanced encoding options.

name: xsstrike2
description: An enhanced version of XSStrike with additional XSS detection techniques, improved DOM analysis, and better handling of single-page applications. It uses context-aware payload generation to achieve higher detection rates with fewer false positives.

name: yawast
description: A web application security auditing tool that checks for common misconfigurations, missing security headers, and known vulnerabilities. It provides a quick overview of a web application's security posture through a comprehensive initial assessment.

name: zap-cli
description: A command-line interface for OWASP ZAP that enables headless scanning, automated testing, and CI/CD integration. It provides access to ZAP's active scanning, spider, and proxy features without requiring the graphical interface.

name: dirsearch
description: Discovers hidden directories and files on web servers by iterating through wordlists with configurable extensions and analyzing HTTP response codes and content lengths. It supports recursive scanning into discovered directories, custom HTTP headers for authentication, and extension fuzzing to find backup files and configuration files.

name: mbenum
description: A web application security enumeration tool that systematically discovers and catalogs information about target systems including users, groups, shares, services, and configurations. It queries the target through legitimate and illegitimate information request channels, HTTP request manipulation, parameter fuzzing, payload injection, and response analysis, building a comprehensive intelligence picture for attack planning.

name: rawler
description: A web application security tool in the BlackArch Linux repository designed for testing web applications for vulnerabilities including injection flaws, authentication bypass, misconfigurations, and data exposure. It operates using HTTP request manipulation, parameter fuzzing, payload injection, and response analysis, providing security professionals with targeted capabilities for assessing and testing web application security infrastructure. Install it via 'pacman -S rawler' and consult the tool's documentation for detailed usage instructions specific to your assessment scenario.

name: waybackurls
description: Retrieves historically archived URLs for a target domain from the Wayback Machine, returning endpoints that may have been removed from the current application but could still be accessible on the server. This often reveals forgotten admin panels, old API versions, debug endpoints, and backup files that are no longer linked but remain functional.

name: gau
description: Collects known URLs for a target domain from passive web archives (Wayback Machine, Common Crawl, URLScan.io) without sending any requests to the target itself. This passive approach avoids detection while revealing endpoints, parameters, and hidden paths that have been previously observed by public web crawlers.

name: qsfuzz
description: A fuzzing tool for web application security that discovers vulnerabilities by sending malformed, unexpected, or random data to target applications and monitoring for crashes, hangs, or abnormal behavior. It generates test cases through mutation or grammar-based strategies, HTTP request manipulation, parameter fuzzing, payload injection, and response analysis, helping identify buffer overflows, injection flaws, and other memory safety issues.

name: log4shell
description: Exploits the Log4Shell vulnerability (CVE-2021-44228) by injecting JNDI lookup strings (${jndi:ldap://...}) into application input vectors that may be logged by a vulnerable Log4j2 instance. When the vulnerable logger processes the string, it connects to the attacker's LDAP server, which returns a malicious Java class that executes on the target.

It achieves throughput of hundreds of thousands of resolutions per second, making it ideal for processing the output of subdomain enumeration tools.

name: slowloris
description: Performs denial-of-service by opening thousands of HTTP connections and keeping them alive through slow, partial request transmission. Each connection sends headers incrementally to avoid timeout detection, gradually consuming the server's maximum concurrent connection limit until legitimate users cannot connect. Requires minimal bandwidth compared to traditional flood attacks.

name: smuggler
description: Detects HTTP request smuggling by sending crafted requests with ambiguous Content-Length and Transfer-Encoding header combinations, then analyzing server responses for evidence that the front-end and back-end servers interpret request boundaries differently. It tests all known smuggling techniques including CL.TE, TE.CL, and TE.TE variants.

name: nmap
description: Nmap (Network Mapper) is the undisputed king of network scanning. It's used for network discovery, security auditing, and vulnerability detection, supporting a vast array of scan techniques, OS detection, service enumeration, and NSE scripting.
how_it_works: Nmap sends raw IP packets to target hosts and analyzes the responses. It supports multiple scan techniques: SYN scan (half-open, stealthy), TCP connect (full handshake), UDP scan, TCP FIN/Xmas/Null scans (bypassing some firewalls), and ACK scan (mapping firewall rules). OS fingerprinting uses TCP/IP stack behavior analysis with over 1,000 signatures. Service detection probes open ports with service-specific payloads. The Nmap Scripting Engine (NSE) extends functionality with 600+ scripts for vulnerability detection, exploitation, and network discovery.
usage_scenarios: Network reconnaissance and host discovery. Service enumeration and version detection. OS fingerprinting for target identification. Vulnerability scanning with NSE scripts. Firewall rule mapping and evasion testing.
strengths: Supports every scan technique and protocol
weaknesses: Can be slow scanning large networks or all ports
SYN scans require root privileges
Some firewalls/IDS detect and block Nmap scans
Stealth scanning is increasingly ineffective against modern defenses
comparison: Masscan is faster for port discovery but lacks service detection. RustScan provides faster port discovery with Nmap integration. Angry IP Scanner offers a GUI alternative. Nmap remains the most feature-rich and versatile network scanner.

name: masscan
description: Masscan is the fastest Internet-scale port scanner, capable of scanning the entire Internet in under six minutes. It achieves this through custom TCP/IP stack implementation and asynchronous transmission.
how_it_works: Masscan uses a custom TCP/IP stack that operates independently of the host operating system's network stack. It transmits SYN packets at extremely high rates (up to 10 million packets per second) using asynchronous I/O. Unlike Nmap which processes one target at a time, Masscan shuffles all target/port combinations and processes them in random order, which distributes the scanning load across targets. When a SYN-ACK is received, it completes the handshake to confirm the port is open, then tears down the connection with RST.
usage_scenarios: Internet-scale port scanning for large-scale reconnaissance. Rapid scanning of large CIDR ranges. Pre-Nmap scanning to identify open ports for detailed enumeration. Bug bounty scanning across massive attack surfaces.
strengths: Custom TCP/IP stack for maximum performance
Randomized scanning order to distribute load
weaknesses: No service detection - only identifies open ports
Can overwhelm targets and network infrastructure
Requires careful rate tuning to avoid packet loss
Less accurate than Nmap due to high-speed packet handling
comparison: Nmap is slower but provides comprehensive service detection. RustScan is faster than Nmap with Nmap integration. ZMap is similar for Internet-scale research scanning. Masscan is the fastest dedicated port scanner.

name: rustscan
description: RustScan is a modern port scanner that uses Rust's async I/O to achieve scanning speeds 10x faster than Nmap. It can scan all 65,535 ports in under 5 seconds, then pipes results to Nmap for detailed service enumeration.
how_it_works: RustScan uses Rust's asynchronous networking to send SYN packets at extremely high rates. It divides the port range into batches and processes them concurrently, utilizing all available CPU cores. Unlike Nmap's sequential scanning, RustScan can probe thousands of ports simultaneously. Once open ports are identified, it can automatically invoke Nmap with service-specific scripts against only the open ports, combining RustScan's speed with Nmap's deep enumeration capabilities.
usage_scenarios: Rapid port discovery during time-sensitive engagements. Pre-Nmap scanning to quickly identify open ports. Large-scale network reconnaissance. Initial footprinting when speed is critical.
strengths: 10x faster than Nmap for port discovery
Seamless Nmap integration for deep scanning
Supports CIDR ranges and target lists
weaknesses: Only does port scanning - no service detection on its own
Can overwhelm firewalls and trigger IDS
Requires ulimit adjustment for full speed
Less stealthy than Nmap's slower scan modes
comparison: Masscan is similarly fast but less accurate and doesn't integrate with Nmap. Nmap is slower but provides comprehensive service detection. RustScan's advantage is its speed combined with automatic Nmap integration.

name: openvas
description: OpenVAS (Open Vulnerability Assessment Scanner) is a full-featured vulnerability scanner that performs comprehensive security auditing of network infrastructure, web applications, and systems. It's the open-source fork of Nessus.
how_it_works: OpenVAS uses a client-server architecture with the scanner daemon (openvassd) performing the actual scanning and the manager (openvasmd) handling scan configuration and results. It maintains a database of over 60,000 Network Vulnerability Tests (NVTs) that are continuously updated. Each NVT is a NASL (Nessus Attack Scripting Language) script that tests for a specific vulnerability. Scans are performed by executing NVTs against targets, with results categorized by severity (Critical, High, Medium, Low, Log).
usage_scenarios: Comprehensive vulnerability assessment of enterprise networks. Compliance scanning for PCI-DSS, HIPAA, and other standards. Regular security auditing of infrastructure. Vulnerability management programs.
weaknesses: Complex installation and setup process
Resource-intensive - requires significant RAM and disk
Scans can take hours for large networks
False positives require manual triage
Web UI can be slow with large result sets
comparison: Nessus is the commercial original with better support. Qualys provides cloud-based scanning as a service. Nexpose (Rapid7) offers similar commercial functionality. OpenVAS is the most capable free vulnerability scanner.

name: nessus
description: Nessus is one of the most widely deployed vulnerability scanners in the world, developed by Tenable. It provides comprehensive vulnerability assessment, configuration auditing, and compliance checking with an extensive plugin database.
how_it_works: Nessus uses a plugin architecture where each plugin tests for a specific vulnerability or misconfiguration. Plugins are written in NASL and updated daily. The scanner performs host discovery, port scanning, service identification, and then runs applicable plugins against discovered services. Results are categorized by severity with detailed remediation guidance. Nessus supports authenticated scanning (using provided credentials) for deeper internal vulnerability assessment.
usage_scenarios: Enterprise vulnerability management. Compliance auditing for regulatory requirements. Pre-deployment security assessment. Continuous vulnerability monitoring.
weaknesses: Commercial product - requires paid license for full features
Resource-intensive scanning engine
Can generate high volumes of network traffic
False positives require manual triage
comparison: OpenVAS is the free alternative with similar architecture. Qualys provides cloud-based scanning. Nexpose offers competitive commercial scanning. Nessus is the industry standard for commercial vulnerability scanning.

name: zgrab2
description: ZGrab2 is a fast, modular Internet-scale application-layer scanner. It's the successor to ZGrab and works alongside ZMap for large-scale service fingerprinting and banner grabbing.
how_it_works: ZGrab2 takes IP:port pairs (typically from ZMap output) and performs application-layer handshakes to collect detailed service banners and certificates. It supports multiple modules (HTTP, HTTPS, SSH, TLS, MySQL, PostgreSQL, Redis, etc.) each implementing protocol-specific probing. Results are output as structured JSON for analysis. The tool uses Go's concurrency model for high-throughput scanning and supports rate limiting to control scan speed.
usage_scenarios: Internet-scale service fingerprinting. TLS certificate analysis across large networks. Banner grabbing for service identification. Large-scale security research and measurement studies.
strengths: Integration with ZMap for full pipeline
weaknesses: Requires ZMap or similar for port discovery
Go binary - not easily extensible
Limited active vulnerability testing
No GUI or visualization
comparison: Nmap provides deeper scanning but is much slower. Masscan only does port scanning. ZGrab2 fills the gap for fast application-layer fingerprinting at scale.

name: amass
description: Amass is the most comprehensive subdomain enumeration tool available. It performs DNS enumeration, scraping, and reverse DNS lookups using over 60 data sources including APIs, web archives, and certificate transparency logs.
how_it_works: Amass operates in multiple phases: DNS enumeration (brute-force subdomains with recursive resolution), scraping (querying search engines, DNS aggregators, and web archives), reverse DNS (finding domains associated with IP ranges), and certificate transparency (parsing CT logs for domain names in certificates). It uses over 60 API sources including Shodan, VirusTotal, PassiveTotal, and more. Results are graphed to show domain relationships, and the tool supports active verification of discovered subdomains.
usage_scenarios: Comprehensive subdomain enumeration for attack surface mapping. Passive reconnaissance without alerting the target. Bug bounty subdomain discovery. Asset inventory and shadow IT discovery.
strengths: Both passive and active enumeration modes
weaknesses: API rate limits on free tiers of data sources
Can be slow with all sources enabled
Requires API keys for full functionality
Large result sets require filtering
comparison: Sublist3r is simpler but less comprehensive. Knockpy focuses on subdomain resolution. Subfinder is faster for passive enumeration. Amass provides the deepest enumeration available.

name: subfinder
description: Subfinder is a fast subdomain discovery tool focused on passive enumeration. It queries multiple data sources simultaneously to find subdomains without sending any requests to the target.
how_it_works: Subfinder uses a purely passive approach, querying external data sources (DNS aggregators, search engines, certificate transparency logs, web archives) for subdomain information. It never sends requests directly to the target domain, making it completely stealthy. The tool uses Go's concurrency to query all configured sources simultaneously, then deduplicates and resolves discovered subdomains. It supports API keys for enhanced results from premium sources.
usage_scenarios: Stealthy subdomain enumeration. Fast passive reconnaissance. Bug bounty subdomain discovery. Asset inventory without alerting target.
strengths: Completely stealthy - no direct target contact
weaknesses: Passive only - misses subdomains not in data sources
Results depend on data source coverage
Requires API keys for best results
Cannot verify if subdomains are currently active
comparison: Amass is more comprehensive with active enumeration. Sublist3r is simpler but slower. Knockpy includes DNS resolution. Subfinder is the fastest passive-only subdomain finder.

name: maltego
description: Maltego is an open-source intelligence (OSINT) and graphical link analysis tool that visualizes relationships between entities like people, organizations, domains, and infrastructure. It's essential for complex reconnaissance.
how_it_works: Maltego uses transforms (plugins) that query data sources and return related entities. Starting from a single entity (domain, person, phone number), transforms discover connected entities: DNS records, WHOIS data, social media profiles, email addresses, document metadata, and infrastructure relationships. Results are displayed as an interactive graph where nodes are entities and edges are relationships. Community edition includes basic transforms, while commercial versions add premium data sources.
usage_scenarios: OSINT investigation and link analysis. Mapping organizational structure and infrastructure. Social engineering reconnaissance. Visualizing complex entity relationships.
commands: # Use transforms to expand entities in the graph
# Export graph as image or report
strengths: Visual graph-based analysis for relationship mapping
Hundreds of transforms for diverse data sources
weaknesses: Community edition has limited transforms
Java-based - resource intensive
Learning curve for effective transform selection
Commercial version is expensive
comparison: SpiderFoot provides automated OSINT with similar data sources. Recon-ng offers a framework-based approach. TheHarvester focuses on email and subdomain discovery. Maltego's advantage is visual relationship mapping.

name: spiderfoot
description: SpiderFoot is an automated OSINT collection tool that gathers intelligence from over 200 data sources. It automates the process of collecting and correlating open-source intelligence about IP addresses, domains, email addresses, and more.
how_it_works: SpiderFoot takes a target (IP, domain, email, username, phone number) and automatically queries over 200 OSINT data sources. It collects DNS records, WHOIS data, breach data, social media profiles, web content, and infrastructure information. Results are correlated to find relationships between data points. The tool includes a web UI for interactive analysis and supports automated scanning with email notifications. It can also integrate with other tools like Shodan and HaveIBeenPwned.
usage_scenarios: Automated OSINT collection for penetration test reconnaissance. Email and credential breach checking. Infrastructure mapping and relationship discovery. Continuous monitoring of target entities.
weaknesses: Can generate overwhelming amounts of data
Many data sources require API keys
Python dependencies can be complex
Some data sources are rate-limited
comparison: Maltego provides visual graph analysis but requires more manual work. Recon-ng is a framework for custom OSINT workflows. TheHarvester is simpler for specific data types. SpiderFoot automates the broadest range of OSINT collection.

name: recon-ng
description: Recon-ng is a full-featured web reconnaissance framework with a modular interface similar to Metasploit. It provides a structured environment for performing open-source intelligence gathering with modules for various data sources.
how_it_works: Recon-ng uses a module-based architecture where each module performs a specific reconnaissance task. Modules are organized by function: discovery (finding hosts, contacts, credentials), enumeration (DNS, ports, paths), exploitation (using discovered information), and reporting (generating output). The framework manages a database of discovered information that modules can read from and write to, enabling data sharing between modules. It supports API key management for premium data sources.
usage_scenarios: Structured OSINT gathering with module-based workflows. Automated reconnaissance pipeline creation. Subdomain and contact enumeration. API-integrated intelligence collection.
strengths: Metasploit-like interface familiar to penetration testers
API key management for premium sources
weaknesses: Learning curve for module selection and chaining
Some modules are outdated or broken
Less automated than SpiderFoot
Database can become unwieldy with large result sets
comparison: SpiderFoot provides more automated collection. Maltego offers visual analysis. Amass is deeper for subdomain enumeration specifically. Recon-ng provides a structured framework for custom workflows.

name: theharvester
description: TheHarvester is a tool for gathering emails, subdomains, hosts, employee names, and URLs from public sources. It's designed for the early stages of penetration testing when broad OSINT gathering is needed.
how_it_works: TheHarvester queries multiple search engines (Google, Bing, DuckDuckGo), social networks (LinkedIn, Twitter), DNS servers, and other public data sources for information related to a domain. It extracts email addresses, subdomains, hostnames, and employee names from search results and API responses. The tool uses rate limiting and rotating user agents to avoid detection. Results are output in a structured format for use in subsequent testing phases.
usage_scenarios: Email harvesting for phishing campaigns. Subdomain discovery for attack surface mapping. Employee name gathering for social engineering. Quick OSINT overview of target organization.
strengths: Multiple search engine and social network sources
Email, subdomain, and employee name collection
weaknesses: Search engine rate limiting reduces effectiveness
Some data sources no longer work reliably
Less comprehensive than SpiderFoot or Amass
Results can be noisy with false positives
comparison: SpiderFoot collects from more sources. Amass is deeper for subdomains. Maltego provides relationship visualization. TheHarvester is best for quick, broad OSINT gathering.

name: shodan
description: Shodan is a search engine for Internet-connected devices. Unlike Google which indexes web content, Shodan indexes the services and devices exposed on the Internet, including industrial control systems, databases, and IoT devices.
how_it_works: Shodan continuously crawls the Internet by performing port scans and banner grabbing on all reachable IP addresses. It indexes the banners, service information, and metadata from discovered services. Users can search for specific device types, software versions, geographic locations, and organizations using Shodan's query syntax. The API enables programmatic access for automation and integration with other tools.
usage_scenarios: Discovering exposed services and devices on the Internet. Finding vulnerable software versions. Identifying industrial control systems and IoT devices. Geographic mapping of Internet infrastructure.
weaknesses: Free tier has limited query quota
Data can be outdated for dynamic services
Cannot access services behind NAT or firewalls
Ethical concerns about device exposure
comparison: Censys provides similar Internet-wide scanning with academic backing. ZoomEye is a Chinese alternative. FOFA is another search engine. Shodan remains the most popular and comprehensive.

name: censys

name: metasploit
description: Provides a modular exploitation framework with thousands of exploit modules targeting vulnerabilities in network services, web applications, and operating systems. Each exploit can be paired with payloads for different outcomes (shell access, meterpreter, DLL injection), and the framework manages sessions, pivoting, and post-exploitation automatically.

name: beef-xss
description: Hooks victim web browsers through XSS vulnerabilities by injecting a JavaScript hook that establishes a persistent connection to the BeEF control server. Through the hooked browser, the attacker can execute JavaScript commands, capture keystrokes and credentials, redirect the victim to phishing pages, and scan internal networks from the browser's perspective.

name: exploitdb
description: Provides an offline searchable archive of thousands of public exploits, shellcode samples, and security papers. Users can search by CVE number, software name, exploit type, or platform to find ready-to-use exploit code, with direct links to the associated vulnerability descriptions and proof-of-concept demonstrations.

name: shellstorm
description: A exploit development and execution access tool that establishes remote shell or persistent backdoor connections to compromised systems. It creates reverse or bind shell connections through various protocols, shellcode injection, ROP chain construction, memory corruption exploitation, and privilege escalation, maintaining access for post-exploitation activities even after initial entry vectors are closed.

name: pwntools
description: Accelerates exploit development by providing Python libraries for TCP/UDP connections, structured data packing, ROP chain building, and binary format parsing. It handles the tedious parts of exploit writing (endianness, padding, address resolution) so researchers can focus on vulnerability logic and exploitation strategy.

name: ropper
description: A exploit development and execution tool in the BlackArch Linux repository designed for developing and deploying exploits against known vulnerabilities, from initial access to post-exploitation. It operates using shellcode injection, ROP chain construction, memory corruption exploitation, and privilege escalation, providing security professionals with targeted capabilities for assessing and testing exploit development and execution infrastructure. description: Extracts Windows credentials from the LSASS process memory including plaintext passwords, NTLM hashes, Kerberos tickets, and DPAPI master keys. It can perform pass-the-hash, pass-the-ticket, and golden ticket attacks, and bypass Windows security features like Credential Guard in certain configurations.

name: impacket
description: Provides Python libraries and scripts for crafting and manipulating network protocols including SMB, LDAP, Kerberos, DCE/RPC, and MSSQL. Its scripts automate common AD attacks including secretsdump (credential extraction), psexec (remote execution), ntlmrelayx (SMB relay), and GetUserSPNs (Kerberoasting).

name: crackmapexec
description: Automates Active Directory post-exploitation by testing credentials across multiple hosts, executing commands via SMB/WMI/WinRM, enumerating shares, sessions, and logged-on users, and dumping hashes through various techniques. It treats the network as a whole rather than individual hosts, enabling rapid lateral movement assessment.

name: evil-winrm
description: Establishes an interactive PowerShell session on Windows targets through the WinRM protocol using stolen credentials or hashes. It supports file upload/download, loading custom PowerShell modules, and bypassing AMSI and script block logging, making it a powerful post-exploitation tool for Windows environments.

name: ligolo-ng
description: Creates transparent network tunnels through compromised hosts using TUN interfaces, enabling direct access to internal networks from the attacker's machine. Unlike SOCKS proxies, it works with any tool without proxy configuration, routing traffic through the pivot host as if the attacker were directly connected to the internal network.

name: chisel
description: Tunnels TCP connections through HTTP, allowing penetration testers to bypass egress filtering and reach internal services. The server runs on a publicly accessible host, the client connects from the restricted network, and TCP ports are forwarded through the HTTP channel. It supports reverse tunneling for accessing services behind NAT.

It combines vulnerability detection with payload delivery to achieve code execution, privilege escalation, or data access, shellcode injection, ROP chain construction, memory corruption exploitation, and privilege escalation, enabling security teams to verify patch effectiveness and assess real-world risk.

name: zaproxy-exploit
description: An exploitation variant of zaproxy that automates the process of exploiting known vulnerabilities in exploit development and execution systems. It combines vulnerability detection with exploit delivery for streamlined penetration testing.

name: john
description: Cracks password hashes by auto-detecting the hash type and applying progressively aggressive attack modes: first dictionary attacks with built-in wordlists, then rule-based mutations of dictionary words (case changes, substitutions, appendages), and finally incremental brute-force for remaining hashes. It supports OpenMP parallelism and GPU acceleration through community patches.

name: hashcat
description: Cracks password hashes using GPU acceleration (OpenCL/CUDA/ROCm) to achieve speeds orders of magnitude faster than CPU-based tools. It supports 300+ hash algorithms and multiple attack modes: straight dictionary, rule-based mutations, combinator (combining two wordlists), hybrid (wordlist + mask), and mask-based brute-force with configurable character sets.

name: hydra
description: Performs online brute-force attacks against network services by testing username/password combinations from wordlists against protocols including SSH, FTP, HTTP form-based authentication, SMB, MySQL, PostgreSQL, RDP, VNC, and dozens more. It supports parallel connections for speed and can resume interrupted sessions.

name: medusa
description: A password cracking and brute-forcing tool in the BlackArch Linux repository designed for recovering passwords from hashes or brute-forcing authentication against network services. It operates using dictionary attacks, rule-based mutations, mask-based brute-force, rainbow table lookups, and GPU-accelerated hash computation, providing security professionals with targeted capabilities for assessing and testing password cracking and brute-forcing infrastructure. Install it via 'pacman -S medusa' and consult the tool's documentation for detailed usage instructions specific to your assessment scenario.

name: ncrack
description: A high-speed network authentication cracking tool from the Nmap project that supports SSH, RDP, FTP, Telnet, HTTP, and many other protocols. It uses a modular architecture with parallel connection handling and optimized timing templates to crack credentials at scale across network services.

name: patator
description: A password cracking and brute-forcing tool in the BlackArch Linux repository designed for recovering passwords from hashes or brute-forcing authentication against network services. description: A password cracking and brute-forcing credential testing tool that recovers passwords or tests authentication strength by systematically trying candidate credentials from wordlists or generated combinations. It applies dictionary attacks, rule-based mutations, mask-based brute-force, rainbow table lookups, and GPU-accelerated hash computation to identify weak passwords that could be exploited by attackers, helping organizations enforce stronger authentication policies.

name: ophcrack
description: Recovers Windows passwords from SAM dumps by looking up NTLM hashes in precomputed rainbow tables rather than brute-forcing each hash. The rainbow tables contain precomputed hash-password pairs that enable instant lookup, cracking most common Windows passwords in seconds even on modest hardware.

name: rainbowcrack
description: Cracks passwords or authentication tokens from rainbow services by applying dictionary attacks and brute-force methods against captured hashes or authentication data. Used to assess password strength on rainbow infrastructure.

name: rcrack
description: A rainbow table-based password cracker that recovers passwords from LM and NTLM hashes using precomputed lookup tables. It trades storage space for cracking speed, performing instant hash lookups in rainbow tables rather than computing hash operations, enabling rapid recovery of Windows passwords.

description: A password recovery tool targeting pkcs12 authentication. It extracts and cracks pkcs12 credentials using dictionary attacks and rule-based mutations, helping security testers identify weak passwords in pkcs12 deployments.

name: cryptohazemulticracker
description: A password cracking and brute-forcing credential testing tool that recovers passwords or tests authentication strength by systematically trying candidate credentials from wordlists or generated combinations. It applies dictionary attacks, rule-based mutations, mask-based brute-force, rainbow table lookups, and GPU-accelerated hash computation to identify weak passwords that could be exploited by attackers, helping organizations enforce stronger authentication policies.

name: aircrack-ng
description: Assesses WiFi network security through a suite of tools: airmon-ng sets the wireless adapter to monitor mode, airodump-ng captures packets and identifies networks, aireplay-ng injects packets to accelerate attacks, and aircrack-ng cracks WEP keys through statistical analysis or WPA/WPA2-PSK keys through dictionary attacks against captured 4-way handshakes.

name: wifite
description: Automates WiFi network auditing by scanning for access points, selecting targets based on signal strength and encryption type, then executing the appropriate attack (WPA handshake capture, WEP injection attack, WPS Pixie Dust). It handles the entire workflow from discovery through cracking with minimal user interaction.

name: kismet
description: Passively monitors 802.11 wireless traffic without transmitting any packets, detecting networks including hidden (non-broadcasting) SSIDs, identifying associated clients, and logging packet captures for forensic analysis. It supports multiple wireless adapters and can simultaneously monitor multiple channels through channel hopping.

name: reaver
description: A wireless network security tool in the BlackArch Linux repository designed for testing WiFi and Bluetooth networks for encryption weaknesses, authentication bypass, and configuration flaws. It operates using packet capture and injection, deauthentication attacks, handshake capture, WEP/WPA cracking, and evil twin deployment, providing security professionals with targeted capabilities for assessing and testing wireless network security infrastructure. Install it via 'pacman -S reaver' and consult the tool's documentation for detailed usage instructions specific to your assessment scenario.

name: autopsy
description: Performs digital forensic analysis of disk images through a GUI built on The Sleuth Kit. It automates ingestion of disk images, extracts deleted files, analyzes file system metadata, reconstructs timelines of file activity, and applies hash filtering to identify known files, providing an integrated workflow for forensic investigators.

name: sleuth-kit
description: Provides command-line forensic utilities for analyzing disk images at the file system level: fls lists files and directories (including deleted ones), icat extracts file contents by inode, ils lists inode structures, and mmls shows volume layout. Together these tools enable detailed investigation of file system structures and data recovery.

name: volatility
description: Analyzes volatile memory dumps to extract forensic artifacts including active processes, loaded DLLs, network connections, registry hives, and cached files. It supports plugins for specific analysis tasks (finding rootkits, extracting passwords, identifying malware) and works with memory images from Windows, Linux, and macOS systems.

name: volatility3
description: A digital forensics tool in the BlackArch Linux repository designed for acquiring, preserving, and analyzing digital evidence from disk images, memory dumps, and network captures. It operates using file system analysis, memory extraction, timeline reconstruction, file carving, and hash verification, providing security professionals with targeted capabilities for assessing and testing digital forensics infrastructure. Install it via 'pacman -S volatility3' and consult the tool's documentation for detailed usage instructions specific to your assessment scenario.

description: A command-line forensic utility from The Sleuth Kit that lists file and directory names from a disk image, including deleted entries, by parsing file system metadata structures for disk image analysis. description: A command-line forensic utility from The Sleuth Kit that extracts file contents by inode number from a disk image, allowing recovery of specific files including deleted ones for disk image analysis. It operates on raw and forensic disk images to extract file system metadata, recover deleted content, and build timelines of file activity for forensic investigations.

name: afl
description: Discovers vulnerabilities through coverage-guided mutation fuzzing: it instruments target binaries to track which code paths each input exercises, then preferentially mutates inputs that reach new coverage areas. By combining lightweight instrumentation with smart input mutation strategies, AFL progressively explores deeper code paths and finds edge cases that trigger crashes and security vulnerabilities.

name: afl-plus-plus
description: A fuzzing and vulnerability discovery tool in the BlackArch Linux repository designed for discovering software vulnerabilities through automated input mutation and coverage-guided testing. It operates using coverage-guided mutation, grammar-based generation, protocol fuzzing, and crash triage, providing security professionals with targeted capabilities for assessing and testing fuzzing and vulnerability discovery infrastructure. Install it via 'pacman -S afl-plus-plus' and consult the tool's documentation for detailed usage instructions specific to your assessment scenario.

name: honggfuzz
description: Fuzzes binaries using coverage-guided mutation with hardware acceleration through Intel Processor Trace for zero-overhead code coverage. It supports persistent fuzzing mode (keeping the target process alive between test cases for speed), multi-process parallel fuzzing, and automatic minimization of crash-inducing inputs.

name: libfuzzer
description: A fuzzing tool for fuzzing and vulnerability discovery that discovers vulnerabilities by sending malformed, unexpected, or random data to target applications and monitoring for crashes, hangs, or abnormal behavior. It generates test cases through mutation or grammar-based strategies, coverage-guided mutation, grammar-based generation, protocol fuzzing, and crash triage, helping identify buffer overflows, injection flaws, and other memory safety issues.

name: setoolkit
description: Automates social engineering attack simulation through modules for spear-phishing (creating and sending emails with malicious attachments or links), web attack (cloning websites for credential harvesting), and payload generation (creating executable payloads that bypass antivirus). It integrates with Metasploit for post-exploitation after successful social engineering.

name: gophish
description: Manages phishing simulation campaigns through a web interface that handles the entire workflow: importing target email lists, creating email templates with tracking pixels, setting up landing pages that capture submitted credentials, and generating analytics reports showing open rates, click rates, and credential submission rates.

name: evilginx2
description: Bypasses two-factor authentication by proxying the victim's login session through a malicious server that sits between the victim and the real authentication service. The victim enters their credentials and 2FA code on what appears to be the legitimate site, while evilginx2 captures the session cookie set after successful authentication, which the attacker can use to access the account without needing 2FA.

name: king-phisher
description: A social engineering phishing tool that creates convincing fraudulent websites or emails to test user susceptibility to social engineering attacks. It clones legitimate login pages, sends templated phishing emails, and captures submitted credentials, phishing campaign automation, credential capture, email tracking, clone site creation, and MFA bypass, providing metrics on organizational phishing awareness.

name: sptoolkit
description: A social engineering tool in the BlackArch Linux repository designed for simulating and testing social engineering attacks including phishing, pretexting, and credential harvesting. It operates using phishing campaign automation, credential capture, email tracking, clone site creation, and MFA bypass, providing security professionals with targeted capabilities for assessing and testing social engineering infrastructure. Install it via 'pacman -S sptoolkit' and consult the tool's documentation for detailed usage instructions specific to your assessment scenario.

description: Extracts Windows credentials from the LSASS process memory including plaintext passwords, NTLM hashes, Kerberos tickets, and DPAPI master keys. name: impacket
description: Provides Python libraries and scripts for crafting and manipulating network protocols including SMB, LDAP, Kerberos, DCE/RPC, and MSSQL. Its scripts automate common AD attacks including secretsdump (credential extraction), psexec (remote execution), ntlmrelayx (SMB relay), and GetUserSPNs (Kerberoasting).

name: bloodhound
description: Visualizes Active Directory attack paths by collecting relationship data (group memberships, session information, ACLs, GPOs) through SharpHound and analyzing it using graph algorithms. It reveals hidden privilege escalation paths that would be impossible to identify manually, showing exactly how an attacker could progress from any starting point to Domain Admin.

name: evil-winrm
description: Establishes an interactive PowerShell session on Windows targets through the WinRM protocol using stolen credentials or hashes. It supports file upload/download, loading custom PowerShell modules, and bypassing AMSI and script block logging, making it a powerful post-exploitation tool for Windows environments.

name: crackmapexec
description: Automates Active Directory post-exploitation by testing credentials across multiple hosts, executing commands via SMB/WMI/WinRM, enumerating shares, sessions, and logged-on users, and dumping hashes through various techniques. It treats the network as a whole rather than individual hosts, enabling rapid lateral movement assessment.

name: ligolo-ng
description: Creates transparent network tunnels through compromised hosts using TUN interfaces, enabling direct access to internal networks from the attacker's machine. Unlike SOCKS proxies, it works with any tool without proxy configuration, routing traffic through the pivot host as if the attacker were directly connected to the internal network.

name: chisel
description: Tunnels TCP connections through HTTP, allowing penetration testers to bypass egress filtering and reach internal services. The server runs on a publicly accessible host, the client connects from the restricted network, and TCP ports are forwarded through the HTTP channel. It supports reverse tunneling for accessing services behind NAT.

name: weevely
description: Weevely is a stealth PHP web shell generator and handler that creates obfuscated backdoors and provides a terminal-like interface for post-exploitation through the web shell. It generates polymorphic PHP payloads that evade signature-based detection.
how_it_works: Weevely generates PHP web shells using polymorphic code generation - each generated shell is syntactically unique but functionally identical, making signature-based detection nearly impossible. The generated PHP uses encoding, variable name randomization, and code obfuscation techniques. Communication between the client and the web shell uses HTTP(S) with encrypted payloads, making traffic analysis difficult. The handler provides over 30 post-exploitation modules for file operations, system command execution, database access, network tunneling, and privilege escalation.
usage_scenarios: Maintaining persistent access to compromised web servers. Post-exploitation through web shells. File management on compromised systems. Network pivoting through web shell proxies.
strengths: Stealthy traffic that blends with normal web traffic
weaknesses: PHP-only - no support for other languages
Web shell must be uploaded to target first
Active scans can detect the shell file
Limited compared to full C2 frameworks like Metasploit
comparison: b374k and WSO are simpler PHP shells without encryption. China Chopper is minimal but widely detected. Metasploit's meterpreter provides more powerful post-exploitation but requires different delivery. Weevely's advantage is stealth and PHP-specific obfuscation.

name: frida
description: Dynamically instruments running applications by injecting a JavaScript V8 engine into the target process, enabling real-time hooking of native function calls, modification of function arguments and return values, and tracing of code execution paths. It works across Android, iOS, Windows, macOS, and Linux, making it the go-to tool for runtime analysis of mobile and desktop applications.

name: objection
description: Automates common mobile security testing tasks through pre-built Frida scripts that can bypass SSL certificate pinning, dump iOS Keychain and Android Keystore contents, enumerate loaded classes and methods, modify runtime behavior, and extract application data. It provides a REPL interface for interactive exploration of running applications.

name: drozer
description: Tests Android application security by deploying an agent on the device that interacts with the Android Inter-Process Communication (IPC) system. It discovers exported activities, content providers, broadcast receivers, and services, then tests each for vulnerabilities including SQL injection in content providers, intent spoofing, and permission bypass.

name: apktool
description: A mobile security tool in the BlackArch Linux repository designed for testing Android and iOS applications for security vulnerabilities including insecure data storage, certificate pinning bypass, and runtime manipulation. It operates using APK/IPA analysis, runtime instrumentation, certificate pinning bypass, and mobile forensics, providing security professionals with targeted capabilities for assessing and testing mobile security infrastructure. Install it via 'pacman -S apktool' and consult the tool's documentation for detailed usage instructions specific to your assessment scenario.

name: chisel
description: Tunnels TCP connections through HTTP, allowing penetration testers to bypass egress filtering and reach internal services. The server runs on a publicly accessible host, the client connects from the restricted network, and TCP ports are forwarded through the HTTP channel. It supports reverse tunneling for accessing services behind NAT.

description: Creates transparent network tunnels through compromised hosts using TUN interfaces, enabling direct access to internal networks from the attacker's machine. Unlike SOCKS proxies, it works with any tool without proxy configuration, routing traffic through the pivot host as if the attacker were directly connected to the internal network.

It places network interfaces in promiscuous mode to capture packets, BLE sniffing, device enumeration, pairing attacks, and protocol fuzzing, enabling credential extraction, protocol analysis, and detection of sensitive data transmitted in cleartext.

name: zigbee-stimulator
description: A Bluetooth security tool in the BlackArch Linux repository designed for testing Bluetooth and BLE devices for vulnerabilities including eavesdropping, spoofing, and unauthorized access. It operates using BLE sniffing, device enumeration, pairing attacks, and protocol fuzzing, providing security professionals with targeted capabilities for assessing and testing Bluetooth security infrastructure. Install it via 'pacman -S zigbee-stimulator' and consult the tool's documentation for detailed usage instructions specific to your assessment scenario.

name: zigbee-fuzzer
description: A fuzzing tool for Bluetooth security that discovers vulnerabilities by sending malformed, unexpected, or random data to target applications and monitoring for crashes, hangs, or abnormal behavior. It generates test cases through mutation or grammar-based strategies, BLE sniffing, device enumeration, pairing attacks, and protocol fuzzing, helping identify buffer overflows, injection flaws, and other memory safety issues.

name: thread-sniffer
description: A Bluetooth security traffic capture tool that intercepts and analyzes network communications between systems. name: chisel
description: Tunnels TCP connections through HTTP, allowing penetration testers to bypass egress filtering and reach internal services. The server runs on a publicly accessible host, the client connects from the restricted network, and TCP ports are forwarded through the HTTP channel. It supports reverse tunneling for accessing services behind NAT.

name: ligolo-ng
description: Creates transparent network tunnels through compromised hosts using TUN interfaces, enabling direct access to internal networks from the attacker's machine. Unlike SOCKS proxies, it works with any tool without proxy configuration, routing traffic through the pivot host as if the attacker were directly connected to the internal network.

name: frp
description: A network tunneling tool in the BlackArch Linux repository designed for creating covert communication channels through restrictive networks by encapsulating traffic within allowed protocols. It operates using DNS tunneling, HTTP tunneling, ICMP tunneling, and SSH tunneling, providing security professionals with targeted capabilities for assessing and testing network tunneling infrastructure. Install it via 'pacman -S frp' and consult the tool's documentation for detailed usage instructions specific to your assessment scenario.

It operates using signal capture and analysis, RF monitoring, protocol decoding, and GPS spoofing, providing security professionals with targeted capabilities for assessing and testing software-defined radio infrastructure. Install it via 'pacman -S soapy-sdr' and consult the tool's documentation for detailed usage instructions specific to your assessment scenario.
